proposals.title: Graph-Structured Prompt Banks: Robust In-Context Learning with Variable Example Counts via Knowledge Graph Integration
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) in large language models (LLMs) is highly sensitive to the number and quality of provided prompt examples, leading to unstable performance in real-world applications where example availability varies. This fragility limits the reliable deployment of ICL for tasks like few-shot tabular reasoning or temporal question answering, where a fixed prompt structure is often assumed. A method to dynamically adapt to and reason over a variable set of in-context examples is needed for robust, real-world use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structuring a variable set of in-context examples as a dynamic knowledge graph, and using a graph neural network to select and weight the most relevant subgraph for a given query, will decouple model reasoning from a fixed prompt length and yield more robust performance across different example counts.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage architecture. First, a 'Prompt Bank' encoder will embed all available task examples (from zero to many) into nodes in a graph, with edges derived from semantic similarity and, for temporal/tabular tasks, structural relations from auxiliary knowledge graphs (inspired by Ren's prior work). Second, a Gated Graph Attention Network (GGAT) will process this graph, and a learned attention mechanism will generate a fixed-size, context-aware representation conditioned on the input query. This 'reasoning context' will be fed to a frozen LLM (e.g., LLaMA) to produce the final answer, enabling dynamic utilization of examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for variable-length in-context learning that improves robustness and sample efficiency. It will extend graph-based reasoning methods to the core LLM prompting paradigm, providing a concrete solution for deployment scenarios with unstable example pools. We will validate performance on temporal (TimeQA) and high-dimensional tabular datasets where auxiliary knowledge is available.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses graphs for knowledge injection or fixed-prompt optimization, dynamically constructing a reasoning graph from a variable prompt set to stabilize ICL performance is novel. It uniquely combines Ren's expertise in graph-based reasoning for tabular/temporal data with the core challenge of prompt engineering robustness.

proposals.title: GraphMini: In-Context Learning on Few-Node Graphs via Auxiliary Knowledge Distillation
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) on graphs is crucial for tasks like molecular property prediction with limited data, but current graph ICL methods struggle with very small graphs or fragments where structural signal is minimal. This limits applications in drug discovery where reasoning about molecular substructures or scaffolds is essential. Pre-2026, methods like TimeGraphs and auxiliary knowledge graphs for tabular data show promise, but are not optimized for the few-node regime.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model pre-trained to perform in-context reasoning by distilling structural and relational knowledge from a large auxiliary graph into a compact, context-aware architecture will significantly outperform standard graph neural networks and language models on few-node graph learning tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop GraphMini, a specialized encoder-decoder. The encoder will be a lightweight, hierarchical graph transformer that processes a few-node input graph alongside retrieved, relevant subgraphs from a large auxiliary knowledge base (e.g., ChEMBL or a general knowledge graph). A knowledge distillation loss during pre-training will force this small encoder to capture relational patterns from the larger auxiliary model. For ICL, the model will be meta-trained on a distribution of few-node graph prediction tasks, learning to adapt its representations from a small set of support examples provided in the context window.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, efficient model for few-shot learning on tiny graphs, enabling practical ICL for molecular fragment analysis. It will extend the principle of auxiliary knowledge graphs, previously used for tabular data with d >> n, to the challenging regime of graph-structured ICL with n (nodes) << 10. We will release a benchmark of few-node graph tasks for evaluating ICL.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly co-designing model architecture and pre-training for the few-node ICL setting, which is underexplored, and in adapting the auxiliary knowledge graph framework—previously for high-dimensional features—to inject structural knowledge for data-scarce, small-graph reasoning.

proposals.title: PromptGraph: Generating In-Context Examples via Model-Guided Graph Search
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) performance is highly sensitive to the choice of prompt examples, but manually curating or searching for optimal examples is expensive and unscalable. Automated methods often treat the model as a black box, ignoring its internal reasoning structure. This work addresses the inverse problem: leveraging a model's learned representations to systematically generate effective prompts, directly linking model introspection to ICL optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: The internal knowledge and reasoning pathways of a trained language model can be explicitly modeled as a graph, and traversing this graph from a query embedding can generate demonstration examples that maximize the model's own performance on that query.
proposals.method: We propose PromptGraph, a method that constructs a knowledge-grounded graph from a model's latent space. For a target query, we encode it and perform a structured search over this graph to retrieve or synthesize candidate demonstration sequences. The search is guided by a learned policy that optimizes for expected downstream task performance, using the model's own predictions as a reward signal. This integrates graph-based reasoning with reinforcement learning, building on Ren's prior work in temporal and tabular knowledge graphs.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a principled, model-aware framework for automatic prompt generation, moving beyond heuristic retrieval. It contributes a novel methodology for 'model introspection for prompting' and could yield significant performance gains in data-scarce or complex reasoning settings where ICL is critical.
proposals.novelty: While prior work retrieves examples from datasets, we generate them by reasoning over a graph derived from the model's internal states. This inverts the typical prompt-engineering paradigm by using the model to explain and optimize its own inputs.

proposals.title: MultiModalBias: A Graph-Based Framework for Probing Social and Temporal Biases in Generative Models Across Text, Audio, and Video
proposals.motivation: Research on bias in AI has primarily focused on unimodal models, especially in vision or text. However, the rapid rise of powerful multimodal generative models (e.g., DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, GPT-4V) for creating and reasoning with text, audio, and video necessitates a systematic, unified framework to audit their biases. A pre-2026 gap exists in applying structured, knowledge-grounded probing methods, proven effective for tabular data, to these complex, high-dimensional generative modalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph-structured knowledge framework, used to probe spurious correlations in tabular models, can be effectively adapted to reveal and quantify latent social (e.g., gender, ethnicity) and temporal (e.g., recency, anachronism) biases embedded in the representations and outputs of multimodal generative models.
proposals.method: We will extend the experimental framework from Ren's prior work on auxiliary knowledge graphs for tabular data. First, we will construct temporal and social knowledge graphs (e.g., ConceptNet, Wikidata) to ground abstract attributes. For each modality (text, audio, video), we will design probing tasks where generative models (e.g., autoregressive LMs, diffusion models) must reason with this graph context. We will measure performance disparities and output distortions across demographic and temporal nodes using metrics like statistical parity, counterfactual fairness tests, and temporal coherence scores, analyzing how the auxiliary graph influences bias mitigation or exposure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, unified benchmarking suite for bias in multimodal generative AI, 2) empirical evidence on how graph-structured knowledge interacts with bias across modalities, and 3) insights into whether auxiliary knowledge graphs, effective for tabular 'd >> n' problems, can act as a bias auditing or correction tool for generative models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in transferring a graph-based bias probing framework from the domain of high-dimensional tabular data to the generative multimodal setting, and in systematically comparing bias manifestations across text, audio, and video generation within a single structured experimental paradigm.

proposals.title: Graph-Guided Compositional Bias Probes: Quantifying Generalization Limits in Vision-Language Models via High-Order Attribute Combinations
proposals.motivation: Modern vision-language models (VLMs) are trained on large-scale datasets, but their ability to generalize to novel, complex combinations of visual attributes (e.g., 'striped metal sphere next to a polka-dotted wooden cube') remains poorly understood. This is critical for real-world applications where object properties interact in intricate ways. Pre-2026, bias probing has often focused on single or pairwise attributes, failing to capture higher-order compositional reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that VLMs exhibit systematic failure modes and spurious correlations when presented with data containing high-order, multi-attribute combinations not seen during training, and that these biases can be formally quantified and mitigated using structured knowledge.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel benchmark dataset by generating synthetic images (e.g., using Blender) where objects are defined by combinations of 3+ attributes (material, texture, shape, color, spatial relation). Inspired by Hongyu Ren's prior work on auxiliary knowledge graphs for tabular data, we will model attribute combinations and their co-occurrence statistics as a temporal knowledge graph (TimeGraphs). This graph will guide the creation of train/test splits that systematically control for the frequency and complexity of attribute combinations. We will then probe pre-trained VLMs (e.g., CLIP, BLIP) on these splits, measuring performance degradation as a function of combinatorial complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a formal framework and benchmark for quantifying compositional bias in VLMs, 2) a diagnostic tool to identify specific high-order attribute combinations that break model reasoning, and 3) insights for creating more robust training data strategies that mitigate these spurious correlations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging knowledge-graph-based structured reasoning—used previously for tabular data—with the systematic probing of high-order visual attribute combinations, moving beyond simple bias audits to a graph-theoretic analysis of generalization failure.

proposals.title: Ecological Rationality Benchmarks for Graph-Augmented Language Models: Evaluating Fitness for Real-World Decision Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for large language models (LLMs) often focus on static knowledge recall or abstract reasoning, failing to capture their fitness for dynamic, resource-constrained real-world decision-making. The principle of ecological rationality from cognitive science—where decision strategies must be evaluated by their fit to environmental structure—provides a powerful, yet underutilized, framework for evaluation. This gap is critical for applications like high-dimensional tabular analysis or temporal reasoning, where models must leverage contextual knowledge efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting language models with structured knowledge graphs will significantly improve their performance on ecological rationality benchmarks, which measure adaptive decision-making under constraints, compared to standard LLMs. This improvement will be most pronounced in tasks where environmental regularities can be encoded as relational or temporal graph structures.
proposals.method: We will design a benchmark suite translating classic ecological rationality tasks (e.g., fast-and-frugal heuristics, sequential search) into NLP formats, emphasizing variable environments and limited input. Building on Hongyu Ren's prior work, we will develop a Graph-Augmented Language Model (GALM) that retrieves and reasons over auxiliary knowledge graphs (e.g., from TimeGraphs or domain-specific sources) to inform its decisions. The model's architecture will integrate a language model backbone with a graph neural network module, using attention mechanisms to fuse contextual text input with relevant subgraph structures. Performance will be evaluated against baseline LLMs on both accuracy and computational efficiency metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating the ecological rationality of generative models, moving beyond static QA; 2) empirical evidence on how structured knowledge integration improves adaptive decision-making in AI systems; and 3) an open-source framework for constructing graph-augmented models for real-world, data-scarce decision tasks where d >> n.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the cognitive science framework of ecological rationality—concerned with strategy-environment fit—to benchmark and improve modern graph-augmented LLMs, a significant departure from standard NLP evaluation paradigms. This creates a direct link between structured knowledge integration and measurable fitness for practical, constrained tasks.

proposals.title: Bias by Design: A Graph-Based Inverse Framework for Inducing Specified Inductive Biases via Synthetic Data Generation
proposals.motivation: Inductive bias is crucial for generalization, especially in data-scarce or high-dimensional (d >> n) settings common in tabular and temporal domains. Current methods rely on model architecture or regularization to impose bias, but these are often indirect and inflexible. A direct, data-centric approach to *designing* bias could offer unprecedented control over model behavior, enabling more robust and interpretable learning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, knowledge-graph-guided generative process can produce synthetic training data that, when used to pre-train or augment a model, reliably instills a pre-specified, beneficial inductive bias, improving performance on downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will formalize the desired bias (e.g., temporal smoothness, hierarchical feature relationships) as constraints within a structured knowledge graph. Building on Hongyu Ren's prior work on auxiliary knowledge graphs for tabular data, we will develop an adversarial inverse optimization framework. A generator network, conditioned on the graph, will produce synthetic tabular or temporal sequences. A learner model trained on this synthetic data will be evaluated, and its learned representations will be compared against the target bias specification via a discriminator, closing the loop for end-to-end training of the data generator.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel inverse framework for data-centric bias design, 2) a concrete methodology integrating knowledge graphs with generative models for controllable data synthesis, and 3) empirical validation on high-dimensional tabular and temporal reasoning datasets, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and generalization.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation and curriculum learning shape learning, directly *solving for* training data that encodes a precise, abstract inductive bias is a largely unexplored inverse problem. The integration of explicit knowledge structures to guide this generation moves beyond purely statistical data synthesis.

proposals.title: Variational Graph Information Bottleneck: Learning Compressed and Robust Representations for Knowledge-Enhanced Reasoning
proposals.motivation: The Information Bottleneck (IB) principle offers a powerful framework for learning compressed, task-relevant representations, but its application to graph-structured data remains challenging, especially for knowledge graphs used in reasoning tasks. Pre-2026, graph neural networks often overfit or fail to generalize when the input graph is noisy or high-dimensional, as seen in tabular learning with auxiliary graphs where d >> n. A principled method to extract minimal sufficient graph representations is needed to improve robustness and interpretability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a variational formulation of the Graph Information Bottleneck (GIB), leveraging the reparameterization trick from Variational Autoencoders, will enable efficient and scalable learning of compressed graph embeddings that are maximally informative for downstream tasks while being invariant to irrelevant noise in the graph structure and node features.
proposals.method: We will develop the Variational Graph Information Bottleneck (VGIB) model. The method involves: 1) Defining a variational upper bound for the GIB objective, using a graph encoder (e.g., GCN or GAT) to parameterize a stochastic latent representation Z. 2) Employing a decoder to reconstruct task-relevant information (e.g., node labels, graph properties) from Z, while using a prior distribution to minimize mutual information with the input graph. 3) Implementing and evaluating VGIB on benchmark graph datasets and the specific high-dimensional tabular learning with auxiliary knowledge graph setting from the researcher's prior work, assessing performance, robustness to noise, and representation compactness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, trainable framework for the Graph Information Bottleneck, making it practical for large-scale knowledge graphs. It will provide a new method for improving generalization and robustness in graph-based reasoning and knowledge-enhanced tabular learning. The work will also offer insights into the trade-off between compression and predictive performance in structured data representations.
proposals.novelty: While VAEs are known for generative modeling and the IB principle is established, their integration into a *variational* objective specifically for graph-structured data to enforce the IB principle is a novel technical synthesis. This moves beyond applying standard VAEs to graphs or using heuristic regularization, offering a principled information-theoretic compression method for graphs.

proposals.title: Graph Information Bottleneck for Inverse Representation Learning: Maximally Informative Graph Summaries
proposals.motivation: Graph representation learning typically focuses on creating embeddings that are predictive of downstream tasks, often discarding information deemed irrelevant. However, for applications in knowledge graph completion, scientific discovery, and model interpretation, we need the inverse: representations that are maximally informative about the original graph structure itself. This is crucial for ensuring that learned representations are faithful and retain the relational context necessary for complex reasoning, especially in high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) tabular settings augmented with graphs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting the Graph Information Bottleneck principle, we can learn graph representations that optimally preserve the mutual information with the input adjacency structure, and that these maximally informative summaries will enable superior performance in tasks requiring reconstruction, reasoning, and knowledge transfer.
proposals.method: We will formalize the objective as maximizing mutual information I(Z; A) between the learned representation Z and the input adjacency matrix A, subject to a constraint on representation complexity. Building on the Information Bottleneck framework for graphs (pre-2026), we will develop a variational lower bound for I(Z; A) and train a graph neural network encoder to maximize it. We will evaluate the method on two fronts: 1) its ability to reconstruct graph topology and attributes, and 2) its utility as a pretraining or regularization strategy for downstream tasks on the tabular datasets with auxiliary knowledge graphs from the researcher's prior work, comparing against standard GCNs, GraphSAGE, and variational graph autoencoders.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled objective for learning self-supervised graph representations that are explicitly information-preserving. It will provide a new tool for creating robust, context-rich graph summaries for tabular data augmentation and temporal reasoning tasks. The resulting models and benchmarks will advance the understanding of fidelity in graph representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While most representation learning aims for compression for a specific task, we explicitly tackle the inverse problem of maximizing input information as a primary objective, creating a 'dual' to the standard Graph Information Bottleneck. This shifts the focus from task-specific compression to general-purpose, information-rich graph summarization.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Graph Learning via Graph Information Bottleneck for Knowledge-Augmented Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Graph learning models typically require abundant labeled data, which is unavailable in many real-world scenarios like rare disease prediction or niche scientific discovery. Few-shot learning on graphs remains a significant challenge, especially when the underlying relational structure is complex and noisy. This work addresses the critical need for models that can generalize from extremely scarce labeled examples by leveraging the inherent structure and auxiliary knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the Graph Information Bottleneck (GIB) principle for few-shot learning will force models to learn minimal, task-relevant subgraph representations, drastically improving generalization and robustness in data-scarce regimes.
proposals.method: We will develop Meta-GIB, a meta-learning framework that integrates GIB into the optimization loop of model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML). For each few-shot task, the inner loop will learn to compress input graphs via a GIB objective, extracting only the subgraph features essential for the task. The outer loop will then meta-optimize the initialization of the GIB-based encoder across tasks, enabling rapid adaptation to new tasks with minimal labels. We will validate on few-shot versions of established graph datasets and knowledge-graph-augmented tabular data benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled, information-theoretic framework for data-efficient graph representation learning. It will provide a novel benchmark for few-shot graph learning and demonstrate how to effectively integrate knowledge graphs as auxiliary context to combat overfitting when labeled data is extremely limited (d >> n).
proposals.novelty: While GIB has been used for robust graph learning and knowledge graphs for augmenting tabular data, its adaptation as a core objective for few-shot meta-learning on graphs is novel. This bridges the gap between information-theoretic graph compression and rapid task adaptation.

proposals.title: Graph Information Bottleneck as a Regularizer for Knowledge Graph Generation and Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Generative models for graphs, especially knowledge graphs, often struggle to produce graphs that are both structurally realistic and semantically meaningful. Existing methods, such as variational autoencoders or graph neural network-based generators, can overfit to training data or generate noisy, implausible structures. The Graph Information Bottleneck (GIB) principle, which seeks a compressed representation that is maximally informative about a target, offers a principled framework for regularization but has not been systematically applied to the generative setting for knowledge-rich graphs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating the GIB principle as a regularizer in a generative model will enforce the learning of a minimal sufficient graph representation, leading to generated graphs with improved structural fidelity, better generalization to unseen relational patterns, and enhanced utility for downstream reasoning tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational graph autoencoder framework where the encoder compresses an input graph into a latent representation. The core innovation is a GIB-based regularizer applied to the latent space, minimizing mutual information between the latent code and the input graph while maximizing its information about a reconstruction target. We will adapt the GIB objective for graphs using recent mutual information estimators. The model will be trained and evaluated on established knowledge graph datasets (e.g., FB15k-237, WN18RR) and benchmarked against state-of-the-art graph generative models on metrics for link prediction, graph property statistics, and performance on downstream tasks like question answering.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, theoretically grounded method for regularizing graph generative models, improving their robustness and generalization. It will provide empirical evidence for the benefits of information-theoretic constraints in graph generation. The resulting models will be particularly valuable for data augmentation in scenarios with limited graph-structured data, such as in tabular learning with auxiliary knowledge graphs, aligning with the researcher's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While GIB has been explored for graph classification and representation learning, its application as a regularizer specifically for the generative modeling and reconstruction of knowledge graphs is novel. This bridges a gap between information-theoretic graph representation learning and generative graph models.

proposals.title: Meta-KG: Few-Shot Adaptation of Knowledge-Enhanced Models via Gradient-Based Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: Knowledge graphs (KGs) are crucial for injecting structured reasoning into language and tabular models, but constructing task-specific KGs is expensive and data-scarce. Pre-2026, methods like Hongyu Ren's auxiliary KG work show performance gains but require significant labeled data for each new graph domain, limiting applicability. Few-shot adaptation to new KGs remains a critical, unsolved challenge for deploying knowledge-enhanced AI in specialized fields like biomedicine or finance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model meta-trained on a distribution of diverse knowledge graph tasks can learn to rapidly adapt its relational reasoning and entity representation modules, enabling strong performance on novel KGs with only a few examples.
proposals.method: We will develop a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework where the base model is a graph neural network (GNN) encoder coupled with a task-specific predictor (e.g., for link prediction or node classification). The meta-training phase will sample many few-shot tasks from a collection of source KGs (e.g., FB15k-237, NELL, WN18RR). The inner-loop optimization will adapt the GNN's final layers and entity projection weights using support sets, while the outer-loop meta-update will learn initialization parameters that facilitate fast adaptation. We will benchmark against fine-tuning and prototypical networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for few-shot knowledge injection into downstream models, reducing the data barrier for KG use. It will yield a benchmark for cross-domain KG adaptation and insights into the transferable structural priors learned by meta-trained GNNs.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to few-shot classification, its targeted use for adapting the *knowledge integration mechanism* itself—specifically the interface between a model and an auxiliary KG—is novel. This bridges Hongyu Ren's work on auxiliary KGs for tabular data with the emerging pre-2026 paradigm of meta-learning for relational data.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Graph Pooling for Multi-Scale Relational Reasoning in Knowledge-Enhanced Tabular Learning
proposals.motivation: Recent work, including by Ren et al., shows that auxiliary knowledge graphs (KGs) can significantly boost performance on high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) tabular prediction tasks. However, existing methods often treat the KG as a static, monolithic context, potentially missing crucial hierarchical substructures and multi-scale relational patterns that are key for complex reasoning. Learning to extract these patterns is a critical, unsolved challenge for robust knowledge integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying hierarchical graph neural network (GNN) pooling techniques to auxiliary knowledge graphs will enable the learning of multi-scale relational substructures, leading to more expressive representations and superior prediction performance on downstream tabular tasks compared to flat GNN aggregation.
proposals.method: We will adapt and evaluate hierarchical graph pooling methods (e.g., DiffPool, SAGPool, ASAP) to process the auxiliary KG. The model will first encode entity nodes via a base GNN, then iteratively pool nodes to create coarsened graphs at multiple resolutions. Learned representations from each hierarchical level will be aggregated and fused with the original tabular features using a cross-attention mechanism. The entire architecture will be trained end-to-end, with the pooling layers learning task-specific substructures crucial for the prediction objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for multi-scale knowledge integration in data-scarce tabular settings. It will provide empirical evidence on how hierarchical graph abstraction improves reasoning over auxiliary knowledge. The resulting model will serve as a strong baseline for structured, knowledge-aware deep learning on tabular data.
proposals.novelty: While graph pooling is studied in general graph classification, its application for learning multi-scale *relational context* to augment another modality (tabular data) is novel. This moves beyond using KGs as flat neighborhood aggregators to dynamically distill them into task-relevant hierarchical schemas.

proposals.title: Relational Attention for Context-Aware Reasoning on Temporal Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: Recent work, including Ren's TimeGraphs and tabular learning with auxiliary knowledge graphs, demonstrates the power of integrating structural knowledge for reasoning. However, existing methods often treat all graph edges and temporal paths uniformly, failing to dynamically weigh the importance of different relational contexts for a specific input query. This limits performance on complex reasoning tasks where only a subset of the available knowledge is relevant.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a transformer-inspired relational attention mechanism into graph-based reasoning models will dynamically weight the importance of different graph paths and contextual edges, leading to more accurate and robust performance on temporal and high-dimensional tabular reasoning tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend architectures like TimeGraphs and tabular models with auxiliary knowledge graphs by integrating a dedicated relational attention module. This module will compute attention scores between the input query (e.g., a tabular data point or a temporal query entity) and the relational context of neighboring nodes and edges in the auxiliary graph. We will adapt multi-head attention to operate over heterogeneous edge types and temporal paths, allowing the model to learn which connections are most salient for prediction. The output will be a contextually weighted graph representation fed into the downstream task-specific layer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable mechanism for making graph-augmented models more context-sensitive. It will advance the state-of-the-art in temporal knowledge graph reasoning and high-dimensional tabular learning by demonstrating significant performance gains on established benchmarks. The proposed relational attention module will be released as a plug-in component for existing graph-based architectures.
proposals.novelty: While attention is common in transformers for sequences, its application to dynamically weight heterogeneous relational paths and temporal edges within an auxiliary knowledge graph for tabular and temporal reasoning is novel. This moves beyond simple graph convolution or static edge weighting used in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: KGraph-Core: A Knowledge-Graph-First Architecture for Language-Guided Tabular Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current methods for tabular deep learning, especially in high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) regimes, treat knowledge graphs (KGs) as auxiliary scaffolds, with text or raw data as the primary modality. This limits the model's ability to leverage the structured, relational reasoning inherent in KGs as a core inductive bias. Inverting this paradigm could lead to more data-efficient and interpretable models for complex tabular tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: By making a knowledge graph the primary, learnable representation and using language models to dynamically ground and augment its nodes and edges with contextual text, we can achieve superior performance and robustness on tabular reasoning tasks compared to text-first or data-first baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-encoder architecture where a Graph Neural Network (GNN) operates directly on a domain-specific KG initialized from structured sources. A lightweight, frozen language model (e.g., a small T5 or BERT variant) processes associated textual metadata or column descriptions to produce feature vectors that augment corresponding KG node embeddings. The core learning occurs via message-passing over this grounded graph. For prediction, we employ a readout function on the refined KG representations, not on raw tabular features. We will adapt techniques from Ren's prior work on auxiliary KGs for the d>>n setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel KG-first neural architecture for tabular data, providing a framework for structured, relational reasoning as a primary process. It will demonstrate empirically that this inversion improves data efficiency and generalization on benchmark tabular datasets. The project will also deliver a methodology for integrating pre-2026 language models as contextual grounding tools for symbolic graph structures.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the inversion of the primary modality: instead of using a KG to regularize a deep tabular model, the KG *is* the core model, with text playing a supporting, grounding role. This fundamentally shifts the reasoning paradigm from feature-based to relation-based.

proposals.title: LatentGraph: Implicit Graph Structure Learning for Contextual Reasoning in High-Dimensional Data
proposals.motivation: Recent work, including by this researcher, has shown that auxiliary knowledge graphs (KGs) significantly boost performance in high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) tabular learning and temporal reasoning tasks. However, constructing and curating explicit, high-quality KGs is expensive, domain-specific, and often a bottleneck. This limits the scalability and general applicability of graph-enhanced models across diverse domains where relational structure is suspected but not formally codified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can jointly learn task-relevant latent graph structures from the input data and context, and that using these implicit structures for reasoning will match or exceed the performance of models reliant on explicit, handcrafted knowledge graphs.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-encoder architecture: a primary encoder (e.g., transformer or deep set network) processes the high-dimensional input features, while a relational encoder infers a latent adjacency matrix over data instances or feature clusters via attention mechanisms or learned similarity functions. This latent graph will dynamically condition message-passing neural network (MPNN) layers within the model. The entire system—relational encoder, graph reasoning layers, and prediction head—will be trained end-to-end using a combination of the primary task loss (e.g., classification) and a sparsity/regularization loss on the latent graph to promote interpretable structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, end-to-end trainable framework for graph-based reasoning without explicit graph supervision, 2) empirical validation on tabular (d>>n) and temporal reasoning datasets demonstrating performance parity with explicit-KG models, and 3) analysis of the learned latent structures, providing insights into implicit data relationships.
proposals.novelty: While graph structure learning exists, its tight integration for *auxiliary* relational reasoning in non-graph data (like tabular or time-series) is novel. It fundamentally relaxes the core constraint of prior KG-augmented work by making the graph a learnable, context-dependent component rather than a fixed input.

proposals.title: Early-Fusion Graph-Language Networks for Enhanced Tabular and Temporal Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current methods for integrating structured knowledge graphs (KGs) with language models (LMs) for tasks like tabular learning (d >> n) or temporal reasoning predominantly rely on late fusion, where modalities are processed separately and combined in final layers. This limits the model's ability to perform deep, joint reasoning from the outset, potentially hindering performance on complex, data-scarce tasks. Early fusion at the input or embedding level could enable more fundamental grounding of language in relational and temporal structures.
proposals.hypothesis: Fusing graph-structured knowledge and language representations at the input or early embedding level, rather than at later layers, will yield more parameter-efficient models with superior reasoning performance on tabular and temporal tasks, especially in high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) regimes.
proposals.method: We will design a novel architecture where input tokens (tabular feature names/values or temporal event descriptions) are initially embedded via a pretrained LM. These embeddings are then immediately fused with structured embeddings from an auxiliary knowledge graph using a relational graph attention network (RGAT). The KG, constructed from domain ontologies or dataset metadata, provides relational constraints. The fused, multi-modal embeddings will serve as the direct input to a downstream transformer or MLP encoder. We will evaluate on tabular datasets from the 'High dimensional...' paper and temporal reasoning benchmarks from TimeGraphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new architectural paradigm for graph-language integration, moving beyond late fusion. It will deliver concrete models and benchmarks demonstrating the efficacy of early fusion for improving data efficiency and reasoning accuracy in tabular and temporal domains.
proposals.novelty: While late fusion of graphs and language is common, systematically exploring and optimizing early fusion for structured data reasoning is novel. This approach directly operationalizes the researcher's prior work on KGs for tabular data and temporal graphs into a new, integrated neural architecture.

proposals.title: Architectural Ablation for Knowledge Fusion: A Systematic Study of GNN Encoders in the GreaseLM Framework
proposals.motivation: GreaseLM (2022) established a powerful paradigm for fusing language model and graph neural network (GNN) representations for reasoning over knowledge graphs and text. However, its architecture fixates on a specific GNN encoder (Relational Graph Convolutional Networks), leaving the impact of the graph encoder choice unexplored. Given the rapid pre-2026 proliferation of GNN architectures—each with distinct inductive biases for capturing structure, compositionality, and long-range dependencies—a systematic evaluation is crucial to understand and optimize this key fusion component for downstream reasoning performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that GNN architectures with stronger capabilities for multi-hop relational reasoning and handling graph heterophily will significantly outperform the baseline R-GCN within the GreaseLM framework, particularly on complex, multi-step QA tasks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled ablation study, integrating diverse pre-2026 GNN architectures as the graph encoder in the GreaseLM fusion pipeline. Specific models to test include: (1) Compositional GNNs (CompGCN) for modeling diverse relation operations, (2) Attention-based GNNs (GATv2) for adaptive neighbor weighting, (3) Heterophily-adapted GNNs (H2GCN), and (4) Message-passing transformers (Graph Transformer). We will benchmark these variants on established knowledge-intensive QA datasets used in GreaseLM (e.g., CommonsenseQA, OpenBookQA) and relational reasoning datasets (e.g., WikiWhy), measuring performance, robustness, and convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of GNN encoder choice in LM-GNN fusion models, yielding actionable architectural guidelines for the community. It will identify which GNN properties are most beneficial for joint reasoning, potentially leading to a new state-of-the-art configuration for knowledge-augmented language understanding.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on *whether* to integrate graphs with LMs, this study rigorously investigates *how* to best encode the graph structurally within a leading fusion framework, moving beyond the default architectural assumption.

proposals.title: Graph-Knowledge Fusion via Cross-Modal Attention for Enhanced Tabular and Temporal Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Recent work has shown that auxiliary knowledge graphs (KGs) can significantly boost the performance of deep learning on high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) tabular data and temporal reasoning tasks. However, existing fusion methods, often based on simple concatenation or linear projections, fail to model the complex, structured relationships between graph entities and textual or numerical context. This limits the model's ability to perform sophisticated joint reasoning, a bottleneck for tasks requiring deep integration of symbolic knowledge and contextual data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying sophisticated, parameter-efficient cross-attention mechanisms, inspired by state-of-the-art vision-language models (VLMs) like BLIP-2 or Flamingo, to fuse graph-structured knowledge with task-specific textual/numerical context will enable more expressive relational reasoning and lead to superior performance on tabular prediction and temporal reasoning benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the cross-modal attention paradigm from VLMs, treating the graph as one modality (encoded via a Graph Neural Network like GAT or GraphSAGE) and the task context (e.g., tabular feature descriptions, temporal query text) as another. A key module will be a lightweight, multi-layer cross-attention transformer where the context tokens attend to the graph node embeddings, and vice-versa, allowing for iterative, fine-grained information exchange. This architecture will be integrated into the pipeline established in Ren's prior work on tabular learning with auxiliary KGs and TimeGraphs, replacing the simpler fusion layers. We will evaluate on established datasets from those papers and new benchmarks requiring complex graph-context reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, generalizable architecture for deep graph-context fusion, 2) empirical evidence demonstrating significant performance gains in tabular and temporal reasoning tasks over prior graph-augmented baselines, and 3) an analysis of how cross-modal attention mechanisms capture interpretable relationships between knowledge graph elements and input data.
proposals.novelty: While cross-attention is established in vision-language domains, its principled application to fuse knowledge graphs with heterogeneous, non-visual context (tabular features, temporal queries) for joint reasoning is novel. This moves beyond treating the KG as a static, separately-processed resource to enabling dynamic, context-dependent knowledge retrieval and integration.

proposals.title: Knowledge-Augmented Tabular Predictors with Neurosymbolic Constraint Solvers
proposals.motivation: Neural models for high-dimensional, low-sample (d>>n) tabular data, as explored in Ren's prior work, often struggle with enforcing domain-specific logical constraints and consistency rules, limiting their reliability and trustworthiness in decision-critical applications. Integrating explicit symbolic reasoning can address this by ensuring predictions satisfy known constraints, a key challenge in pre-2026 neural-symbolic AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a neural tabular predictor, guided by an auxiliary knowledge graph, with a differentiable logical constraint solver will yield more accurate, consistent, and data-efficient models by explicitly enforcing hard relational and temporal constraints during learning and inference.
proposals.method: We will extend the architecture from 'High dimensional, tabular deep learning with an auxiliary knowledge graph' by integrating a neurosymbolic reasoning layer. This layer will use a differentiable satisfiability (SAT) or answer set programming (ASP) engine, such as `torchsat` or `DL2`, to process the neural model's initial predictions and knowledge graph embeddings. The system will be trained end-to-end, with the neural component learning feature representations and the symbolic component applying constraints (e.g., temporal consistency from TimeGraphs, functional dependencies) to refine outputs, using a loss that penalizes constraint violations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid architecture for constrained tabular deep learning that guarantees output consistency with domain knowledge. It will provide a methodological framework for transferring logical reasoning engines into data-scarce predictive tasks, advancing the field of trustworthy and interpretable machine learning for structured data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of a differentiable symbolic reasoner directly into the training loop of a knowledge-graph-augmented tabular deep learning model, creating a transferable neurosymbolic framework for the d>>n regime, which is underexplored compared to vision or NLP applications.

proposals.title: CurriculumCLUE: Curriculum Learning for Complex Graph-Text Reasoning in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) struggle with complex, multi-step reasoning that requires integrating structured knowledge from graphs with unstructured text, a core challenge for tasks like scientific QA and complex fact verification. Pre-2026 methods often train on mixed-complexity datasets, which can lead to suboptimal performance and poor generalization on the hardest examples. Drawing inspiration from curriculum learning in education, where concepts are introduced in increasing order of difficulty, offers a promising but underexplored avenue to systematically improve reasoning performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structuring the training of a language model with a curriculum of increasingly complex graph-text reasoning tasks will lead to more robust, data-efficient learning and superior final performance on complex reasoning benchmarks compared to standard training regimes.
proposals.method: We will design a curriculum over a hybrid graph-text dataset (e.g., a modified version of CLUE or TimeQA), defining complexity metrics based on graph density, path length between entities, and textual query ambiguity. The model, a graph-augmented transformer (building on Ren's prior work with auxiliary knowledge graphs), will be trained in stages. We will implement and compare automatic curriculum scheduling strategies, including Self-Paced Learning and Teacher-Student Progress Matching, to dynamically sequence training instances from simple factual lookup to complex multi-hop temporal and relational reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel curriculum learning framework specifically tailored for graph-text reasoning and a new benchmark with annotated complexity scores. It will provide empirical evidence on how training order impacts the acquisition of complex reasoning skills in LLMs, advancing beyond simple dataset mixing.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning has been applied in vision and simple NLP, its systematic application to the hybrid graph-text domain, with complexity defined jointly over structure and language, is novel. This bridges the gap between educational theory and state-of-the-art knowledge-enhanced language model training.

proposals.title: KAGN: Knowledge-Augmented Graph Networks for Few-Shot Temporal and Tabular Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Real-world applications in domains like healthcare or finance often involve complex tabular or temporal data where labeled examples for specific reasoning tasks (e.g., predicting rare event sequences) are extremely scarce. Pre-2026, large language models (LLMs) struggle with structured data reasoning without extensive fine-tuning, and pure graph-based methods require substantial task-specific data. A method that leverages auxiliary knowledge to enable learning from few or zero examples is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly structuring auxiliary domain knowledge into a graph and using it to condition a meta-learning or prompt-based inference process, a model can achieve robust performance on diverse temporal and tabular reasoning tasks with only a few training examples per task type.
proposals.method: We will extend the auxiliary knowledge graph framework from Ren's prior tabular work and the temporal representations from TimeGraphs. The core architecture will be a dual-encoder: a graph neural network (GNN) to encode the auxiliary knowledge graph, and a transformer to encode the input (tabular row or temporal sequence). For few-shot learning, we will employ a meta-learning (e.g., MAML) setup where the knowledge graph provides a shared, structured prior across tasks. For zero-shot, we will develop a retrieval-augmented method where the knowledge graph guides the generation of instructional prompts for a frozen LLM.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified framework for data-efficient reasoning on structured data, demonstrating significant performance gains over standard fine-tuning in sub-10-shot regimes. It will yield a publicly available benchmark for few-shot structured reasoning, combining temporal and tabular tasks, and offer insights into how explicit knowledge graphs complement parametric knowledge in LLMs.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning and knowledge graphs exist separately, their integration for structured data reasoning—specifically bridging temporal dynamics and high-dimensional tabular contexts—is unexplored. The novel meta-learning strategy conditioned on a retrievable knowledge graph, rather than just data embeddings, moves beyond standard parameter adaptation.

proposals.title: Graph Property Emergence: Learning to Predict Macroscopic System States from Auxiliary Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: Current graph learning predominantly focuses on node classification or link prediction, which are microscopic tasks. However, many real-world decisions in science and industry require predicting emergent, macroscopic properties of an entire system, such as the stability of a financial network, the phase of a material, or the toxicity of a molecular compound. Pre-2026 methods for such global graph property prediction are often limited to simple graph-level pooling or handcrafted features, lacking a principled way to integrate rich, auxiliary knowledge that could inform the system's holistic state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained to explicitly integrate auxiliary knowledge graph context with the primary data structure can significantly outperform existing methods in predicting emergent global graph properties, especially in data-scarce (d >> n) tabular or structured domains.
proposals.method: We will extend the auxiliary knowledge graph framework from Ren's prior work. The primary input (e.g., a tabular dataset with d >> n) will be linked to an auxiliary knowledge graph (KG). Instead of using the KG to refine node/edge representations for downstream tasks, we will design a two-stage graph-readout architecture. First, a relational graph neural network (RGNN) will encode the fused primary-KG structure. Second, a novel hierarchical attention-based pooling mechanism will iteratively condense this representation, explicitly modeling the emergence of global properties from local interactions and KG context. The final graph-level embedding will be used for property prediction via a multi-layer perceptron.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable framework for predicting emergent properties in complex systems by inverting the traditional graph learning focus. It will deliver new benchmark results for global property prediction on datasets from materials science, computational social science, and bioinformatics. The methodology will advance the integration of symbolic knowledge into geometric deep learning for system-level reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing the auxiliary KG—previously used for feature refinement—as a scaffold for explicitly modeling property emergence, coupled with a dedicated readout architecture designed for macroscopic prediction rather than as a simple post-processing step.

proposals.title: Graph-Structured Attention: Replacing Conditional Expectation Factorization with a Temporal Knowledge Graph Prior
proposals.motivation: Transformer-based models rely on the attention mechanism, which often assumes a factorized conditional expectation structure for computational tractability. This factorization can oversimplify complex, structured dependencies in data, particularly in temporal and relational reasoning tasks. Pre-2026 work, such as TimeGraphs, shows that explicit graph-based temporal reasoning can significantly enhance model performance, suggesting that the standard attention approximation is a limiting factor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that approximating the attention distribution using a probabilistic graphical model, specifically a temporal knowledge graph prior derived from auxiliary structured knowledge, will capture richer conditional dependencies and lead to more robust reasoning than the standard factorized approach.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel attention layer where the query-key compatibility score is conditioned on a dynamic, auxiliary knowledge graph. This involves: 1) constructing a temporal knowledge graph from the input context and external knowledge bases; 2) using a Graph Neural Network (GNN) to learn node embeddings that encode relational and temporal constraints; 3) integrating these embeddings into the attention calculation via a structured probabilistic model (e.g., a conditional random field) that modulates the standard dot-product attention, thereby challenging its naive factorization. We will evaluate on datasets from tabular (d >> n) and temporal reasoning domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, more expressive family of attention mechanisms that explicitly incorporate structured knowledge. It will provide a principled method to integrate graph-based reasoning directly into the core transformer architecture, potentially improving performance on tasks requiring complex relational and temporal inference. The resulting model will bridge the gap between purely data-driven attention and structured knowledge representation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly replacing the factorized conditional expectation assumption in attention with a learnable graphical model prior, moving beyond simple positional or semantic biases. This integrates Hongyu Ren's expertise in auxiliary knowledge graphs for tabular data with the core attention mechanism, a non-obvious synthesis given that most pre-2026 improvements to attention focus on efficiency or local windows rather than its fundamental probabilistic structure.

proposals.title: GraphCombiner: Scaling Sparse Attention to Arbitrary Graph Structures for Efficient Long-Range Reasoning
proposals.motivation: The Combiner attention mechanism provides a powerful factorization for efficient long-sequence modeling, but its application is largely confined to 1D sequences. Many critical domains, such as molecular modeling, knowledge graph reasoning, and spatiotemporal forecasting, involve data with inherent 2D/3D or graph topology. Scaling Transformer-based reasoning to these complex structures without prohibitive computational cost remains a key challenge in pre-2026 deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Combiner factorization can be generalized to arbitrary graph structures by leveraging the graph's adjacency to define a hierarchical, sparsity-inducing attention mask, enabling efficient, full-graph attention that scales sub-quadratically with the number of nodes.
proposals.method: We will develop GraphCombiner, a novel attention layer where the 'routing' of the Combiner's global tokens is determined by a multi-hop adjacency matrix derived from the input graph. For each node, local attention will be applied to its direct neighbors, while global attention will be computed over a learned, small set of 'anchor' nodes selected via a graph clustering algorithm (e.g., METIS). We will integrate this layer into a Graph Transformer backbone and evaluate it on benchmarks for molecular property prediction (OGB), temporal knowledge graph reasoning (using datasets like ICEWS), and long-range graph classification.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a theoretically grounded, scalable attention mechanism for graph-structured data, 2) a new state-of-the-art for tasks requiring full-graph context over large, dense graphs, and 3) a bridge between efficient sequence modeling and geometric deep learning, expanding the applicability of Transformer architectures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses heuristics like neighborhood sampling or fixed random walks for graph attention, GraphCombiner provides a principled, learnable factorization based on the Combiner framework, offering guaranteed sparsity and a direct mechanism for capturing long-range dependencies in graph topology.

proposals.title: Set2Box: Geometric Deep Learning on Answer Sets for Enhanced Knowledge Graph Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Knowledge graph (KG) reasoning often involves predicting a set of correct answers (e.g., tail entities for a given query). Existing embedding-based methods like box embeddings model answer sets as geometric regions but struggle to capture complex, non-uniform internal structures of these sets. Pre-2026, geometric deep learning on sets offers powerful, permutation-invariant architectures for modeling such collections, yet they remain underexplored for structured knowledge reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling answer sets directly as unordered point clouds via set-based neural architectures will better capture their relational structure and ambiguity, leading to more expressive and accurate KG reasoning compared to static geometric region embeddings.
proposals.method: We will develop Set2Box, a model that represents each query's answer set as a latent point cloud. A Deep Sets or Set Transformer architecture will process this set, conditioned on the query embedding, to produce a refined representation. This representation will be used to score candidate answers, either by measuring membership via a learned function or by comparing against a generated prototype set. We will integrate this with a base box embedding model (e.g., from Query2Box) and evaluate on standard KG completion datasets (e.g., FB15k-237) and high-dimensional tabular tasks with auxiliary KGs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, set-theoretic paradigm for KG reasoning that moves beyond fixed geometric regions. It will provide a framework for learning the internal geometry of answer sets, potentially improving performance on complex, multi-answer queries. The method also extends the applicability of geometric deep learning to structured knowledge problems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of permutation-invariant set networks to model answer sets in KGs, a departure from modeling them as monolithic boxes or vectors. This combines Hongyu Ren's expertise in graph-based reasoning and high-dimensional learning with cutting-edge (pre-2026) set-based architectures.

proposals.title: DynamicBox: Continuous Learning of Box Embeddings for Evolving Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: Knowledge graphs (KGs) in real-world applications are dynamic, with new facts constantly emerging. Static embedding models like TransE or ComplEx require costly full retraining upon updates, which is inefficient and fails to capture temporal dependencies. This work addresses the critical need for KG embedding methods that can adapt continuously to new information without catastrophic forgetting, enabling applications like real-time recommendation and question answering over evolving knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a box embedding framework, augmented with a neural memory buffer and a temporal regularization loss, can enable effective continuous learning on KGs by efficiently incorporating new facts while preserving the structural integrity of previously learned embeddings.
proposals.method: We will extend the box embedding paradigm (e.g., BoxE) for continuous learning. A neural memory buffer will store a subset of past facts (entities and relations) based on graph centrality metrics. For each batch of new facts, the model will be trained with a combined loss: a standard box embedding loss for the new data and a distillation loss on facts retrieved from the memory buffer to mitigate forgetting. We will evaluate on temporal KG benchmarks like ICEWS14/18 and a dynamic version of Wikidata, measuring performance on both new and old facts over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, parameter-efficient method for continuous KG embedding that maintains high performance on both historical and new data. It will provide a benchmark for evaluating dynamic KG reasoning and demonstrate how geometric embeddings (boxes) are particularly suited for incremental learning due to their inherent capacity for representing nested hierarchies and logical constraints.
proposals.novelty: While box embeddings have been explored for static KGs, their application to continuous learning is novel. The proposed method uniquely combines the geometric interpretability of boxes with a replay-based continual learning strategy tailored for graph-structured data, moving beyond simple fine-tuning or naive retraining.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Interface Programs for Stable Neural-Symbolic Coordination in Knowledge-Intensive Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Hybrid neural-symbolic systems promise to combine the pattern recognition of foundation models with the structured reasoning of symbolic engines. However, pre-2026 work struggles with unstable training and poor credit assignment across the non-differentiable interface, limiting their application to complex, knowledge-intensive tasks like temporal reasoning over graphs. A robust, learnable interface is needed to enable seamless coordination.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned, parameterized Interface Program (IP) can be optimized to provide stable gradient signals and facilitate efficient credit assignment across the neural-symbolic boundary, enabling end-to-end learning of hybrid systems for superior performance on tasks requiring joint perception and reasoning.
proposals.method: We will design an IP as a structured module (e.g., a differentiable program or a neural network with syntactic constraints) that translates between neural activations and symbolic queries for a reasoner like a graph-based temporal logic engine. Drawing on pre-2026 meta-learning (e.g., MAML) and Hongyu Ren's expertise in graph-based learning, we will co-optimize the neural foundation model and the IP using a meta-learning outer loop. This loop will train the IP to produce interface states that lead to stable gradient-based updates for the neural component, even when the symbolic reasoner is non-differentiable.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel meta-learning framework for training heterogeneous neural-symbolic systems, 2) a concrete implementation of a constrained Interface Program for robust translation, and 3) empirical validation on challenging datasets requiring integration of learned representations (e.g., from language models) with symbolic reasoning over knowledge graphs.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses fixed or hand-designed interfaces, our IP is meta-learned explicitly for stable gradient flow and coordination. This moves beyond simply making components differentiable and instead optimizes the communication protocol itself for end-to-end learning efficacy.

proposals.title: DEANSR: Differentiable Edge Architectures for Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning via Interface Learning and Graph-Based Distillation
proposals.motivation: Deploying foundation models on edge devices is hindered by their massive scale and lack of reliable, verifiable reasoning. Current neuro-symbolic approaches treat symbolic modules (e.g., solvers, KG traversals) as black-box APIs, creating a brittle integration that is not optimized end-to-end for compactness. This limits the creation of small, trustworthy models for real-time reasoning in constrained environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the input-output interfaces and control logic of non-differentiable symbolic modules as differentiable, learnable components within a neural architecture, we can co-design and distill ultra-compact hybrid models that achieve robust, data-efficient reasoning suitable for edge deployment.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a small neural network (e.g., a compact transformer or MLP) learns to produce latent representations that serve as soft, differentiable inputs to a symbolic module (like a KG query or constraint solver). We will use straight-through estimators and REINFORCE to propagate gradients through the discrete operations. Building on Hongyu Ren's expertise, we will employ graph neural networks to encode knowledge graph context and use this to guide the distillation of a large teacher model's reasoning capabilities into the small hybrid system, focusing on datasets where d >> n.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training framework for creating ultra-compact neuro-symbolic models and demonstrate their performance on resource-constrained reasoning tasks. It will provide a new paradigm for scaling down models by embedding efficient symbolic reasoning, moving beyond compressing pattern recognition.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses symbolic modules as fixed subroutines, we propose to learn their integration interfaces and control logic directly via gradients, enabling true co-design. This approach specifically targets the d >> n, edge-deployment scenario not addressed by large-scale neuro-symbolic systems.

proposals.title: Adversarial Graph-Based Benchmarking for Continuous Discovery of Knowledge and Reasoning Failures in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Static benchmarks for large language models (LLMs) are quickly saturated, failing to expose novel failure modes as models improve. This creates a false sense of performance and leaves critical gaps in safety and reasoning unassessed. An automated, adaptive system is needed to continuously generate challenging evaluations that evolve with the target model's capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: An adversarial ecosystem, where probe models are iteratively refined via reinforcement learning to exploit a target LLM's weaknesses, will generate a living benchmark that uncovers emergent failure modes more effectively than static datasets.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-agent system where probe models (e.g., specialized LMs, program synthesizers) generate inputs targeting specific failure metrics (e.g., logical consistency, factual grounding). These probes will be trained using a graph-structured knowledge base, informed by the researcher's prior work on auxiliary knowledge graphs for tabular data, to guide adversarial input generation towards semantically valid but challenging regions. A reinforcement learning loop, with rewards based on the target model's failure severity, will continuously refine the probes, creating a dynamic benchmark generator.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a framework for continuous, automated model evaluation that outputs not just a test set but an adaptive benchmark generator. It will also yield a structured, evolving map of failure modes, providing actionable diagnostics for model improvement in knowledge and reasoning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static adversarial attacks or human-curated benchmarks, this integrates graph-guided knowledge constraints with a closed-loop RL system to automate the discovery of *semantically coherent* yet systematically challenging failures, creating a self-improving evaluation ecosystem.

proposals.title: Verification-Aware Data Curation: A Graph-Guided Synthesis Framework for Provable Model Safety
proposals.motivation: Current AI safety verification often struggles with models trained on broad, unconstrained data, where the learned behavior is difficult to formally bound. This is critical in high-assurance domains like healthcare, where a model's reasoning must adhere to strict domain rules. A promising but underexplored approach is to co-design the training data with the verification process to ensure the model's knowledge base inherently satisfies formal preconditions.
proposals.hypothesis: By first encoding domain safety specifications into a formal knowledge graph and then using it to guide the synthesis or curation of training datasets, we can train models whose adherence to safety properties is more amenable to complete and robust formal verification.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where: 1) Domain safety rules are formalized as constraints in a temporal knowledge graph (extending TimeGraphs concepts). 2) This graph is used as an auxiliary structure to guide a data synthesis engine, generating 'verification-aware' tabular or sequential training examples that satisfy the graph's logical and temporal constraints. 3) A model (e.g., a GNN or tabular deep learning architecture enhanced with this graph) is trained on this curated data. 4) Formal verification methods (e.g., abstract interpretation or SMT-based bounded verification) are applied, leveraging the known alignment between the data distribution and the formal preconditions to simplify and strengthen the proof.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for creating high-assurance AI training pipelines, specifically a technique for generating verification-friendly datasets. It will also provide a concrete case study demonstrating improved verification completeness and robustness for a model in a safety-critical domain, compared to training on generic data.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is inverting the typical verification workflow: instead of verifying a model post-hoc, we curate the training data to be 'verification-ready' from the start, using a formal knowledge graph as the bridge between domain specifications and data synthesis. This tight integration of knowledge-guided data curation and formal methods is largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: K-Energy: Knowledge-Graph Constrained Optimization for Efficient and Robust Tabular Learning
proposals.motivation: The push for energy-efficient AI often leads to models that sacrifice robustness and fairness, creating critical safety risks in high-stakes applications. Pre-2026, methods for adversarial robustness and bias mitigation are typically added as separate, energy-intensive regularization terms, creating a complex trade-off landscape. There is a lack of frameworks that formally guarantee minimum thresholds for these safety properties while directly minimizing computational energy costs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating energy-efficient training as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem, with verifiable performance, adversarial accuracy, and fairness bounds as constraints, we can produce models that are simultaneously efficient, robust, and equitable. Furthermore, we posit that an auxiliary knowledge graph can guide this optimization to navigate the high-dimensional tabular space (d >> n) more effectively.
proposals.method: We will develop a constrained optimization loop where the primary objective is a proxy for energy consumption (e.g., FLOPs or model sparsity). Constraints will be enforced via Lagrangian multipliers for adversarial accuracy (evaluated on PGD-attacked data) and bias metrics (e.g., demographic parity difference). Building on Hongyu Ren's prior work, we will use an auxiliary knowledge graph to structure the high-dimensional feature space, informing a novel graph-regularized loss that shapes the feasible region. Optimization will use a modified primal-dual learning algorithm with adaptive constraint scheduling.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel constrained optimization framework for guaranteed efficient-robust-fair model design, 2) a new graph-informed loss function for navigating this constrained space in tabular domains, and 3) empirical benchmarks on financial and healthcare datasets showing Pareto improvements over sequential regularization baselines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal constraint-based guarantee for safety properties during energy minimization, moving beyond soft trade-offs, and the integration of auxiliary knowledge graphs to make this constrained optimization tractable for high-dimensional tabular data.

proposals.title: EvoSparse: Generating Specialized Model Ecosystems via Modular Duplication and Task-Driven Pruning
proposals.motivation: The computational cost of deploying large foundation models for diverse downstream tasks is prohibitive. While pruning and distillation create smaller models, they often produce a single, generalist model or lose crucial task-specific performance. A method is needed to efficiently spawn a family of high-performance, specialized models from a single foundation model, analogous to how biological systems evolve specialized functions from a common ancestor.
proposals.hypothesis: That key functional modules (e.g., attention heads, feed-forward layers) within a transformer-based foundation model can be identified, duplicated, and then selectively sparsified for distinct tasks, creating a set of efficient, specialized models that retain interoperability and outperform monolithic pruning approaches.
proposals.method: We will leverage the researcher's expertise in graph-based reasoning to model the foundation model's computation as a dynamic knowledge graph, where nodes represent neurons or attention pathways and edges represent influential interactions. Using this graph, we will identify critical, transferable modules for duplication. For each target task, we will apply structured, lottery-ticket-style pruning to the duplicated modules, guided by a task-specific performance loss and a graph-based regularizer that preserves the core, shared knowledge structure. The pruning process will be formulated as a constrained optimization over the duplicated sub-network.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, biologically-inspired paradigm for model specialization, moving beyond single-model compression. It will provide a practical framework for generating a performant, memory-efficient ecosystem of task-specific models from one foundation model. The method also offers a new perspective on model interpretability by revealing which modular substructures are conserved or specialized across tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard pruning which reduces a single model, our approach explicitly duplicates and diverges modules, enabling true functional specialization. It uniquely combines concepts from evolutionary biology (subfunctionalization) with graph-based structural analysis and targeted sparsification, a synthesis not explored in pre-2026 model efficiency literature.

proposals.title: Meta-Graph: A Unified Meta-Learning Framework for Cross-Task Knowledge Transfer on OGB-LSC
proposals.motivation: The Open Graph Benchmark Large-Scale Challenge (OGB-LSC) presents diverse tasks (node, link, graph-level) across distinct datasets, typically tackled with isolated, single-task models. This paradigm ignores potential shared structural and semantic knowledge, leading to inefficient learning and poor generalization on data-scarce tasks. A unified approach that leverages the collective context of these tasks could improve performance and data efficiency, advancing towards more generalizable graph AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework that explicitly models and transfers relational and temporal knowledge patterns across diverse OGB-LSC tasks will outperform single-task baselines, particularly on tasks with limited labeled data, by learning a more robust and generalizable initialization.
proposals.method: We propose a meta-graph neural network (Meta-GNN) framework using a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) paradigm adapted for heterogeneous graph tasks. The 'meta-knowledge' will be a shared GNN encoder, potentially enhanced with temporal reasoning modules inspired by TimeGraphs, initialized via meta-training on a subset of OGB-LSC tasks. For each new task, the model will perform fast adaptation. Crucially, we will construct an auxiliary, cross-dataset knowledge graph linking entities and relation types across different OGB-LSC datasets to guide the meta-learning process and provide a structured prior.
