proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical meta-learning framework for the heterogeneous OGB-LSC benchmark, demonstrating the value of cross-task knowledge transfer in graph ML. It will provide new insights into what structural knowledge is transferable across different graph learning problems. The proposed auxiliary knowledge graph for cross-dataset relations will be a novel resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored for graphs, applying it to the extreme heterogeneity and scale of OGB-LSC tasks is novel. The key innovation is the integration of an explicitly constructed cross-dataset knowledge graph to constrain and inform the meta-learning process, bridging Hongyu Ren's expertise in auxiliary knowledge graphs with multi-task learning.

proposals.title: InverseGraph: Generating Auxiliary Knowledge Graphs from Predictive Model Explanations for Tabular Data
proposals.motivation: Auxiliary knowledge graphs (KGs) have proven effective for improving deep learning on high-dimensional, low-sample-size (d >> n) tabular data, as shown in Ren's prior work. However, constructing such graphs often relies on expensive, domain-specific external knowledge, limiting scalability and applicability. This project addresses the inverse problem: can we generate plausible, task-relevant graph structures directly from a trained predictive model, thereby automating and democratizing this powerful approach?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the feature interactions and relational structures learned implicitly by a high-performing tabular deep learning model can be explicitly extracted and formalized into a synthetic knowledge graph, and that this generated graph can be used to effectively regularize or enhance similar predictive tasks on novel, related datasets.
proposals.method: We will leverage Ren's established framework for tabular learning with auxiliary KGs. First, we train a state-of-the-art tabular model (e.g., FT-Transformer) on a source dataset. We then apply post-hoc explanation methods (e.g., integrated gradients, attention weights) to quantify pairwise feature interactions as candidate edges. These are filtered and structured using graph learning techniques to form a probabilistic adjacency matrix. Finally, we validate the generated graph by using it as the auxiliary input for training a new model on a different but related target dataset, assessing performance gains against baselines without graphs or with manually curated graphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, automated pipeline for generating auxiliary knowledge structures from data and models, reducing dependency on external knowledge bases. It will advance the understanding of how relational priors are captured in tabular deep learning and offer a practical method for transferring learned structural knowledge across related prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While graph generation is a known problem, generating task-specific auxiliary graphs for tabular data regularization from model explanations is unexplored. It inverts the established paradigm of using a known graph to improve predictions, instead deriving the graph from the predictions themselves.

proposals.title: MultiModal-KG: Joint Pretraining of Knowledge Graphs, Language, and Vision for Cross-Modal Tabular Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Real-world reasoning tasks often involve heterogeneous data sources, including structured tables, unstructured text, and visual inputs like product images or medical scans. Pre-2026, methods like Hongyu Ren's auxiliary knowledge graph (KG) framework have shown promise for high-dimensional tabular learning, but they are largely limited to text and symbolic knowledge. Extending this to a truly multimodal joint pretraining framework is crucial for building AI systems that can reason over the full spectrum of human-centric data, a key challenge in fields like e-commerce, healthcare, and scientific discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified pretraining objective, which aligns representations from images, text, and an auxiliary knowledge graph into a shared embedding space, will significantly improve performance on downstream tabular reasoning tasks that involve multimodal inputs, compared to modality-specific or late-fusion baselines.
proposals.method: We will extend the auxiliary KG framework for tabular data (d >> n) by integrating a vision transformer (ViT) encoder for images. A multimodal encoder (inspired by architectures like VL-BERT or LXMERT, pre-2026) will process aligned (image, text) pairs, while a separate graph neural network (GNN) encodes the auxiliary KG. A key technical innovation will be a novel cross-modal alignment loss that enforces consistency between visual concepts, their textual descriptions, and corresponding entities in the KG. We will pretrain on datasets like MIMIC-CXR (medical images+reports) or Amazon product data (images+descriptions+tabular specs), using masked modality modeling and contrastive learning objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, scalable framework for joint pretraining of tabular data, knowledge graphs, and images; 2) a new benchmark for evaluating multimodal tabular reasoning; and 3) empirical evidence demonstrating that explicit KG grounding mitigates modality gap issues and improves data efficiency in multimodal learning for structured prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While multimodal vision-language pretraining is established, its integration with a structured knowledge graph specifically for enhancing reasoning over high-dimensional tabular data (where d >> n) is a novel and non-trivial fusion, moving beyond late fusion or treating tables as mere text.

proposals.title: AutoFuse: Neural Architecture Search for Graph-Augmented Multimodal Fusion in Tabular Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: In high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) tabular learning, auxiliary knowledge graphs (KGs) provide crucial relational context to improve performance, as shown in Ren's prior work. However, the optimal architecture for fusing tabular features with KG embeddings is highly dataset-dependent and currently designed manually, limiting robustness and scalability. Automating this fusion design is a critical step towards making graph-augmented tabular deep learning more widely applicable and performant.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural architecture search (NAS) framework can discover fusion architectures that outperform manually designed baselines by dynamically adapting the fusion strategy—such as early, late, or hierarchical integration—to the specific structure of the tabular data and its auxiliary knowledge graph.
proposals.method: We will develop a differentiable NAS (DNAS) framework over a search space encompassing key fusion operations: attention-based feature modulation, gated multimodal units, and graph neural network propagation layers. The search space will be explicitly parameterized to include decisions on where and how to inject KG-derived embeddings into the tabular deep learning pipeline. The search will be guided by a performance objective on validation data, utilizing one-shot NAS techniques (e.g., DARTS) for efficiency, and evaluated on established d >> n tabular benchmarks with auxiliary KGs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a general, automated framework for designing high-performance fusion modules in graph-augmented tabular models, 2) empirical insights into what fusion strategies work best for different data-KG relationships, and 3) new state-of-the-art results on relevant benchmarks by optimizing architecture for the task.
proposals.novelty: While NAS has been applied to vision and language, its use for automating the fusion of tabular data with auxiliary knowledge graphs is novel. This moves beyond manual architecture design and static fusion, introducing a data-aware, learnable integration paradigm for this hybrid reasoning domain.

proposals.title: MoE-GRAPH: Scaling Multi-Modal Tabular Reasoning with Mixture-of-Experts and Auxiliary Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning for tabular data, especially in high-dimensional (d >> n) scenarios, struggles to integrate heterogeneous auxiliary knowledge (e.g., temporal relations, entity graphs) efficiently. Scaling model capacity naively to handle multiple data modalities and complex reasoning tasks leads to prohibitive computational costs and risks overfitting on limited datasets. A method is needed to scale capacity selectively and efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: A Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, conditioned on input data and a retrieved context from an auxiliary knowledge graph, can dynamically scale model capacity for tabular learning, improving performance on downstream reasoning tasks while maintaining parameter efficiency and mitigating overfitting.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture where a sparse MoE layer is integrated into a transformer-based tabular deep learning model. For each input instance, a lightweight router network will activate a subset of experts. The routing weights will be conditioned not only on the raw tabular features but also on a context vector retrieved from an auxiliary knowledge graph (as in Ren's prior work). This graph will encode temporal and relational constraints (TimeGraphs). Experts will specialize in different data regimes (e.g., sparse vs. dense features) or reasoning patterns (e.g., temporal extrapolation, relational inference).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable framework for multi-modal tabular reasoning that demonstrably improves performance on high-dimensional datasets without proportional compute overhead. It will provide new insights into how conditional computation and structured knowledge interact to guide expert specialization in heterogeneous data settings.
proposals.novelty: While MoE is known in language modeling, its application to conditional computation for tabular data guided by a knowledge graph context is novel. The integration specifically targets the d >> n problem by allowing the model to specialize and scale capacity based on both data statistics and external relational knowledge.

proposals.title: Robust Policy Learning from Suboptimal Demonstrations via Temporal Knowledge Graph Alignment
proposals.motivation: Learning from demonstrations (LfD) is crucial for complex tasks, but real-world datasets often contain noisy, suboptimal, or even adversarial trajectories, which degrade policy performance. Pre-2026 methods struggle to robustly disentangle underlying task logic from demonstrator-specific biases or deceptions. This problem is acute in high-dimensional, data-scarce domains where auxiliary knowledge is needed for generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating temporal reasoning over an auxiliary knowledge graph with sequence modeling can isolate the core task structure from suboptimal demonstration patterns, enabling robust policy learning even from deceptive or low-quality data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream architecture: 1) A temporal graph encoder (extending TimeGraphs) processes demonstration sequences as paths on a task-relevant knowledge graph, capturing temporal dependencies and logical constraints. 2) A transformer-based policy network processes raw state-action sequences. A contrastive alignment module will force consistency between the graph-derived task representation and the policy's latent space, penalizing deviations attributable to known suboptimal patterns (e.g., inefficiencies, deceptive loops). The model will be trained on datasets like RoboNet or adversarial variants of Atari datasets, using a combined imitation and graph-consistency loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel LfD framework robust to demonstration quality, advancing the integration of symbolic knowledge graphs with deep policy learning. It will provide new benchmarks for learning from adversarial demonstrations and offer theoretical insights into separating task semantics from demonstrator bias.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using temporal knowledge graphs not just as a static auxiliary input, but as an active reasoning module to temporally ground and critique demonstration sequences, a departure from prior inverse reinforcement learning or behavioral cloning approaches that lack such structured, temporal world knowledge.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Agent Task Decomposition with Knowledge Graph Context
proposals.motivation: Real-world multi-agent tasks, such as collaborative logistics or complex game strategies, are long-horizon and require decomposing high-level goals into coordinated sub-tasks. Pre-2026 methods often struggle with sample efficiency and credit assignment in such settings. This research addresses the gap by leveraging structured knowledge to guide the decomposition and learning process, improving reasoning over long action sequences.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) structure with an auxiliary knowledge graph for context will enable more efficient imitation and generalization in long-horizon, multi-agent tasks by providing a structured prior for sub-task decomposition and agent coordination.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-level HRL framework where a high-level manager policy, conditioned on a task-specific knowledge graph, selects sub-goals or sub-tasks. Low-level worker policies, potentially per agent, execute actions to achieve these sub-goals. The knowledge graph, constructed from domain data (e.g., from tabular datasets or temporal logs as in Ren's prior work), will provide relational constraints and state abstractions. We will adapt algorithms like Option-Critic or HIRO, using graph neural networks to encode the knowledge context for the manager policy, and train the system via imitation learning on expert demonstrations of multi-agent tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework that combines HRL with explicit knowledge representation for multi-agent coordination. It will advance methods for sample-efficient learning in complex, structured environments and demonstrate how auxiliary knowledge graphs can guide hierarchical policy learning, extending beyond static tabular data to dynamic multi-agent scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a contextual knowledge graph into the manager level of an HRL framework for multi-agent tasks is novel, moving beyond using graphs solely for feature enrichment to actively guiding temporal sub-task decomposition and inter-agent reasoning.

proposals.title: Graph-Structured Agents: Policy Learning via Dynamic Communication Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) often struggles with scalability and non-stationarity in complex environments. Pre-2026, a key challenge is enabling agents to reason about dynamic relationships and share context efficiently for coordinated action. This work addresses the need for structured agent policies that can explicitly model and adapt to evolving inter-agent dependencies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parameterizing agent policies with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) over a dynamic communication graph will enable more effective, scalable, and generalizable multi-agent coordination by explicitly learning to reason over structured agent relationships.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where each agent's policy network is a GNN layer. The input is a dynamic graph where nodes are agents with local observations, and edges represent communication links, updated via an attention mechanism. We will use a GNN architecture like Graph Attention Networks (GATs) or Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) to perform iterative message passing, aggregating contextual knowledge from neighbors. This structured policy will be trained end-to-end with reinforcement learning (e.g., PPO or MADDPG) on multi-agent benchmarks such as StarCraft II or multi-robot coordination tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, general framework for structured agent policies that explicitly leverages relational inductive biases. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and final performance on cooperative MARL tasks. Furthermore, it will provide insights into how learned communication graphs evolve and facilitate transfer to unseen agent configurations.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been used for environment modeling, their direct use as the core parameterization of a multi-agent policy, with a graph that is both a communication medium and a learned representation of agent relationships, is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond fixed interaction architectures or simple pooling, enabling dynamic, context-aware reasoning.

proposals.title: Dual-Graph: Adversarial Demonstration Augmentation for Robust In-Context Learning via Knowledge Graph Discriminators
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) with demonstrations is highly sensitive to the quality and relevance of provided examples, leading to unstable performance. Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Ren's research on knowledge graphs (KGs) for tabular data and temporal reasoning, shows that auxiliary structured knowledge can stabilize learning in data-scarce regimes. This project addresses the critical need for robust ICL by intelligently curating demonstration sets.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-model framework, where a discriminator trained on a task-aligned knowledge graph adversarially filters or augments demonstration examples, will produce more robust and generalizable in-context learning policies for language models than standard demonstration selection.
proposals.method: We propose a GAN-inspired framework. A generator (the policy LLM) performs ICL. A discriminator is a Graph Neural Network (GNN) trained on a task-specific knowledge graph (constructed from domain corpora or datasets like TabFact) to classify demonstration quality. This discriminator adversarially scores candidate demonstrations, filtering out noisy examples or generating hard negatives for augmentation. The policy LLM is then fine-tuned or prompted using the refined demonstration set, with the process iterating to improve robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel adversarial training paradigm for demonstration curation in ICL, 2) a method to integrate explicit knowledge graphs into the demonstration selection loop, and 3) empirical evidence on tabular and temporal reasoning tasks showing improved robustness to demonstration noise and distribution shift.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and knowledge graphs exist separately, their integration into a *demonstration augmentation loop* for ICL is novel. The key innovation is using a KG-based discriminator as a learned, structured prior for judging demonstration utility, moving beyond simple retrieval or random selection.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Guided Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning via Knowledge Graph Abstraction
proposals.motivation: Training multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) systems for complex, long-horizon tasks is notoriously difficult due to sparse rewards, non-stationarity, and exponential growth in joint action spaces. Pre-2026 methods often struggle with sample efficiency and fail to learn sophisticated coordination. A principled curriculum that structures the learning experience from simple to complex is a promising but underexplored pathway to scalability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum learning strategy, where task difficulty is progressively increased based on a structured abstraction of the environment's relational dynamics, will lead to significantly faster learning, higher final performance, and more robust emergent policies in complex multi-agent settings compared to flat training.
proposals.method: We will formalize the environment and agent capabilities as a temporal knowledge graph (TimeGraphs), where nodes are entities/agents and edges are relations or possible actions over time. This graph will guide curriculum generation: simple subgraphs define initial tasks (e.g., isolated agent-object interactions). Using graph metrics (e.g., connectivity, path complexity), we will automatically propose progressively more complex sub-tasks, expanding the context and required coordination. MARL agents (e.g., using MADDPG or QMIX) will be trained on this sequence, with their learned policies serving as input for higher-difficulty stages, effectively applying 'tabular deep learning with an auxiliary knowledge graph' principles to the policy space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a general framework for generating curricula for MARL using knowledge graph abstraction, 2) a novel integration of temporal relational reasoning with progressive policy learning, and 3) empirical demonstration of superior sample efficiency and performance on benchmark multi-agent environments (e.g., StarCraft II, Overcooked) and a new complex logistics simulator.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using a dynamic knowledge graph not just as an auxiliary input for a single model, but as an explicit, automatable map for structuring an entire multi-agent training curriculum, directly linking relational environment structure to learning progression.

proposals.title: Causally Regularized Beta Embeddings via Adversarial Independence Constraints for Robust Tabular and Graph Learning
proposals.motivation: In high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) tabular and graph-structured data, deep learning models often exploit spurious correlations, leading to poor generalization and uninterpretable representations. Pre-2026 methods, including auxiliary knowledge graphs, integrate external structure but do not explicitly enforce causal, semantically meaningful independence between learned embedding dimensions. This limits robustness to distribution shifts and interpretability of the learned factors.
proposals.hypothesis: Imposing a Structural Causal Model (SCM) prior on Beta distribution embeddings and adversarially penalizing violations of specified conditional independencies will yield representations where correlations are causally structured, improving out-of-distribution generalization and interpretability for downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where Beta distribution parameters (alpha, beta) for each entity are generated by an encoder. A user-specified SCM prior defines groups of dimensions that should be independent given latent concepts. An adversarial predictor will be trained to predict the task label using only a masked version of the embedding that contains information violating these independence constraints. The primary encoder will be trained to maximize task performance while minimizing the adversary's success, using a gradient reversal layer. We will test this on tabular datasets with d >> n and on temporal knowledge graphs (extending TimeGraphs), using the auxiliary graph to inform the SCM prior.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel regularization framework for learning causally structured, distributional embeddings, enhancing model robustness and interpretability. It will provide a new method to integrate domain knowledge (as an SCM) into representation learning for tabular and graph data, moving beyond correlation to causation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the adversarial enforcement of *conditional independence constraints* derived from an SCM prior within a Beta embedding space, a combination not explored in pre-2026 graph or tabular deep learning literature.

proposals.title: Learning Correlated Beta Embeddings for Domain-Specialized Knowledge Graph Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Probabilistic embeddings, like Beta distributions, effectively model uncertainty in knowledge graphs (KGs) but are typically trained on broad datasets, enforcing an assumption of dimension independence. For specialized domains (e.g., biomedicine, materials science), this ignores critical ontological correlations, limiting the model's ability to capture nuanced, domain-specific relationships and reasoning patterns. This gap is significant for high-dimensional, low-sample (d >> n) tabular learning tasks where an auxiliary KG is used, as seen in Hongyu Ren's prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: Training Beta distribution embeddings on a curated, domain-specific KG will yield a learned covariance structure between embedding dimensions that is both interpretable and superior for downstream reasoning tasks, compared to embeddings trained on a universal KG which enforce near-independence.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Beta Embedding framework (e.g., BetaE) and train two models: one on a broad KG (e.g., Wikidata) and one on a curated domain KG (e.g., a biomedical ontology). We will explicitly parameterize and regularize the full covariance matrix of the Beta distributions. Using Ren's expertise in graph-based temporal reasoning and tabular learning with auxiliary KGs, we will evaluate on domain-specific tasks like temporal link prediction and as a prior for a d >> n tabular deep learning model, analyzing the learned correlations for alignment with domain ontology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for learning domain-adapted probabilistic embeddings with correlated dimensions, directly improving reasoning performance in specialized fields. It will yield a diagnostic framework for evaluating the epistemic structure captured by embedding covariances, advancing interpretable KG representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While Beta embeddings exist, explicitly learning and exploiting correlated dimensions tailored to a domain's ontology is unexplored. This moves beyond independence assumptions to model the epistemic structure of a field directly within the embedding space.

proposals.title: Meta-Learned Sparse Routing for Compositional Neural Operators on Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: Neural methods for reasoning over knowledge graphs (KGs) and tabular data often learn monolithic, task-specific operators, which lack interpretability and fail to generalize compositionally to new logical queries. This is a critical limitation for building robust systems that can perform complex, multi-hop reasoning, as seen in prior work on temporal and high-dimensional relational data. A method that learns stable, reusable reasoning primitives could enable more systematic generalization and prevent catastrophic forgetting across tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that separating the learning of fixed, interpretable neural operator primitives from their query-specific, sparse composition via meta-learning will yield a system that generalizes more robustly to novel compositional tasks while maintaining performance on seen ones.
proposals.method: We will define a core library of small, fixed neural modules, each implementing a basic logical operation (e.g., projection, intersection, negation). A meta-learning framework, inspired by MAML, will train these modules over a distribution of KG reasoning tasks to be universally useful. For a new query, a lightweight, adaptive router network—conditioned on the query and context—will learn to select and sparsely combine a relevant subset of these primitives via a gating mechanism, dynamically constructing the required composite operator without updating the core modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for compositional reasoning that explicitly decouples primitive learning from dynamic composition. It will provide empirical evidence for improved out-of-distribution generalization on KG query answering benchmarks and offer a more interpretable model of neural logical operations through the sparse activation of modules.
proposals.novelty: While modular networks exist, the integration of meta-learning to train stable, logic-inspired primitives combined with a learned sparse router for dynamic, query-specific composition is a novel approach for knowledge graph reasoning, moving beyond static architectures or fully adaptive networks.

proposals.title: Adaptive Logical Operators for Knowledge Graph Reasoning via Attention-Based Gating
proposals.motivation: Neural models for reasoning over knowledge graphs, such as Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and query embedding methods, often rely on fixed, pre-defined logical operators (e.g., projection, intersection). This rigidity limits their ability to handle the diverse and context-sensitive semantics of real-world queries, especially in low-data or high-dimensional (d >> n) settings where auxiliary knowledge is critical. An adaptive operator mechanism could improve generalization and compositional reasoning by dynamically reconfiguring its computational primitives based on the specific query and its context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing neural logical operators as dynamic, query-specific combinations of learned primitive functions, governed by an attention-based gating mechanism, will yield more flexible and powerful reasoning models than architectures with fixed operators, leading to superior performance on complex knowledge graph query answering tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a library of simple, parameterized neural modules representing primitive logical operations (e.g., set-like projections, unions). For each input query, a multi-head attention gating network will compute a soft selection vector over this library, generating a context-adaptive composite operator. This framework will be integrated into a recurrent query reasoning architecture over knowledge graphs. We will evaluate the model on standard datasets (e.g., FB15k-237, NELL) and in the challenging d >> n tabular learning regime, using auxiliary knowledge graphs as in the researcher's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, flexible architecture for neural symbolic reasoning that moves beyond fixed operator schemes. It will provide empirical evidence for the benefits of adaptive computation in knowledge-intensive tasks and offer a new method for integrating contextual signals into logical reasoning pipelines, potentially improving performance on complex queries.
proposals.novelty: While attention is widely used for feature weighting, applying it as a gating mechanism over a library of fundamental neural logical operators to create dynamic, query-specific reasoning functions is a novel architectural concept. It directly challenges the standard design of static operators in neuro-symbolic models.

proposals.title: KNOwledge-Guided Operator Synthesis (KNOgS): Learning Domain-Specific Neural Operators via Graph-Curated Data
proposals.motivation: Current neural models, especially for high-dimensional, low-sample (d>>n) domains like biomedicine or law, rely on generic operators (e.g., standard attention, convolution) that lack intrinsic domain alignment. This leads to poor sample efficiency and opaque reasoning. Pre-2026 work shows auxiliary knowledge graphs (KGs) can boost tabular deep learning, but they only provide static feature context, not dynamic architectural guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can learn to synthesize novel, domain-specialized neural operators by conditioning its architectural search on a dynamically curated, task-relevant subgraph, leading to superior performance and interpretable reasoning structures compared to fixed-operator models.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a curation module, inspired by graph reasoning methods like in TimeGraphs, extracts a task-specific subgraph from a large domain KG (e.g., Hetionet for bio, a legal statute graph). Second, a hypernetwork, conditioned on this subgraph's relational and ontological structure, generates the weights and connectivity patterns of a small set of core, task-dedicated neural operators (e.g., specialized attention heads, message-passing layers). These synthesized operators form the core of a predictive model trained end-to-end on the target domain's tabular or sequential data.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel paradigm for domain-adaptive architectures where operators are synthesized, not just selected. 2) A concrete methodology merging knowledge graph curation with hypernetworks for operator generation. 3) Empirical validation on d>>n benchmarks, demonstrating gains in performance and sample efficiency over prior KG-augmented and standard deep learning baselines.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond using knowledge graphs as static auxiliary inputs to using them as blueprints for generating the model's fundamental computational units, creating a tight, data-driven feedback loop between domain structure and architectural plasticity.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Fusion of Probabilistic and Textual Embeddings for Robust Knowledge Graph Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Knowledge graph (KG) embeddings like BetaE capture relational uncertainty but lack rich semantic context, while language model (LM) embeddings provide semantics but are not structurally grounded for KG reasoning. This semantic-structural gap limits performance on complex tasks like temporal or few-shot reasoning, where both logical consistency and contextual nuance are critical. A principled method to fuse these complementary representations, akin to sensor fusion in robotics, is needed to create more robust and interpretable KG models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treating probabilistic KG embeddings and textual LM embeddings as uncertain sensor modalities and fusing them with a learned, uncertainty-aware neural module will produce hybrid embeddings that significantly outperform single-modality baselines on complex KG reasoning tasks, while providing explicit confidence estimates.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural fusion module inspired by probabilistic filtering (e.g., Kalman filters). For each entity/relation, BetaE provides a probabilistic embedding (mean, concentration) and an LM (e.g., BERT) provides a contextual vector. The module will learn to compute modality-specific uncertainty scores, using these to perform a weighted combination via attention. A gated recurrent or transformer-based update network will iteratively refine the fused representation, ensuring structural consistency from BetaE and semantic enrichment from the LM. We will evaluate on temporal (e.g., YAGO11k) and high-dimensional tabular datasets with auxiliary KGs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, uncertainty-aware fusion framework for multi-modal KG representations, 2) hybrid embeddings that enable more robust reasoning on complex queries, and 3) explicit confidence scores for downstream decision-making, enhancing model interpretability. It directly extends Dr. Ren's work on graph-based temporal reasoning and tabular learning with auxiliary KGs.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit adaptation of probabilistic sensor fusion principles (e.g., uncertainty-weighted updates) to the embedding fusion problem, moving beyond simple concatenation or averaging. This provides a principled, learnable mechanism to balance structural and semantic signals based on their estimated reliability.

proposals.title: BetaE-Scale: Distributed Training and Inference for Box Embeddings on Billion-Edge Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: Knowledge graph reasoning models like BetaE, which embeds entities and relations as Beta distributions in box spaces, have shown strong performance on complex logical queries, including negation and union. However, their application is limited to small-scale graphs due to high memory and computational costs. Scaling these models to web-scale KGs like Freebase or Wikidata (with billions of edges) is crucial for real-world applications in search, question answering, and data integration, but remains an unsolved challenge in pre-2026 literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a distributed training and inference framework with novel graph partitioning and synchronized embedding update strategies, the BetaE model can be effectively scaled to billion-edge knowledge graphs without significant degradation in reasoning performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed system building on PyTorch Distributed and DGL. The core technical approach involves: 1) A relation-aware graph partitioning algorithm to minimize cross-partition edges and balance computational load. 2) A distributed negative sampling strategy that operates efficiently across partitions. 3) A synchronized parameter server architecture for the Beta distribution embeddings, optimized for the communication patterns of box intersection/union operations. 4) A batched inference pipeline for answering complex queries that span multiple graph partitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, distributed framework for training and evaluating BetaE on billion-edge graphs, enabling its use on real-world datasets. It will provide a systematic analysis of the scalability-accuracy trade-offs for box embedding models and establish new benchmarks for large-scale KG reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While distributed training exists for simpler KG embeddings like TransE, adapting it for the geometrically complex operations of BetaE (box intersections, unions) and for complex query reasoning is a novel and non-trivial engineering and research challenge not addressed in prior work.

ideas.title: Fuse Probabilistic and Textual Embeddings via Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of multi-modal sensor fusion from robotics to knowledge graph representation learning. The original concept of fusing BetaE's probabilistic embeddings with language model embeddings is analogous to fusing data from a LIDAR (providing geometric, probabilistic structure) and a camera (providing rich textual/visual context) in robotics. The analogous solution uses probabilistic filters to combine uncertain sensor data. Adapted here, we propose a neural fusion module that treats each embedding type as a sensor with an associated confidence or uncertainty measure. This module learns to perform a weighted, probabilistic combination of the two vectors, potentially using techniques like attention over uncertainty scores or a learned Kalman-like update, resulting in a hybrid embedding that is both structurally grounded and semantically informed, with explicit confidence estimates for downstream reasoning tasks.

ideas.title: Domain-Specialized Beta Embeddings with Correlated Dimensions
ideas.description: This research investigates whether training Beta distribution embeddings on highly curated, domain-specific data yields superior performance by allowing the model to learn meaningful correlations between embedding dimensions that reflect the unique ontological and epistemic structure of the target field. We hypothesize that broad, universal datasets enforce an implicit prior of dimension independence that is suboptimal for specialized tasks, whereas curated data enables the discovery of critical covariance structures in the probabilistic embedding space. The core experiment would compare models trained on broad vs. specialized data, explicitly measuring and evaluating the learned correlation matrices of the Beta embeddings for their interpretability and impact on downstream task performance within the domain.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: generate prompts from a model
ideas.description: Given a trained model and a query, generate effective prompt examples for in-context learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: PRODIGY: Enabling In-context Learning Over Graphs

ideas.title: Causally Regularized Beta Embeddings via Adversarial Independence Constraints
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework to learn Beta distribution embeddings where the correlations between dimensions are causally structured and semantically meaningful. Instead of passively modeling all correlations, we impose a structural causal model as a prior, specifying which groups of dimensions should become independent conditioned on latent concepts. An adversarial module attempts to predict the task output using only information that violates these conditional independence constraints, thereby penalizing spurious correlations. This results in embeddings whose interdependencies are robust, interpretable, and generalizable beyond the training distribution.

ideas.title: Generative Adversarial Benchmarking for Continuous Failure Mode Discovery
ideas.description: This research designs a dynamic evaluation ecosystem where benchmark generation is an ongoing, automated process. A set of adversarial probe models, which may include language models, program synthesizers, or image generators, is tasked with creating inputs that cause a target foundation model to fail according to specified metrics (e.g., consistency, safety, reasoning). These probes are iteratively refined through reinforcement learning, using the target model's failures as a reward signal, ensuring the benchmark evolves in tandem with model improvements and novel capabilities. The system outputs not just a static test set, but a living benchmark generator and a continuously updated map of emergent failure modes.

ideas.title: Adaptive Neural Operators via Attention-Based Gating
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that neural operators for logical operations must be architecturally fixed. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience models of selective attention—where dynamic gating reconfigures information pathways based on task demands—we propose making neural operators adaptive per query. The solution is to implement an attention-based gating mechanism that, for each input query, computes a soft selection or combination over a learned library of basic operator primitives. This allows the model to learn context-sensitive logical operations, moving from a rigid, pre-wired architecture to a flexible, query-adaptive one, potentially improving generalization and compositional reasoning in neural networks.

ideas.title: Domain-Adaptive Neural Operators via Specialized Data Curation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the co-design of adaptive neural operator architectures and the specialized data that trains them. Instead of using a fixed, universal set of operators on broad data, the system learns to reconfigure its core logical operations (the neural operators) by being exposed to a curated, domain-specific dataset. The curated data dictates not just the parameters but the very structure and connectivity of the operators, leading to a model whose reasoning primitives are intrinsically aligned with the ontological and causal relationships of a specific field (e.g., legal reasoning, biochemical interaction). This yields a novel paradigm where superior performance stems from a tight feedback loop between domain-aware architectural plasticity and high-precision data curation.

ideas.title: Differentiable Edge Architectures for Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning (DEANSR)
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to co-design ultra-compact foundation models with integrated, non-differentiable symbolic reasoning modules (e.g., constraint solvers, theorem provers, knowledge graph traversals) for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. The core innovation is a training and distillation framework that treats these symbolic components not as black-box APIs, but as architectural elements whose interfaces and control logic are optimized through gradient-based learning in the neural substrate, even if their internal operations are discrete. This enables the creation of small models that are inherently verifiable, data-efficient, and capable of complex, reliable reasoning tasks currently requiring orders of magnitude more parameters, fundamentally shifting the goal of scaling down from compressing pattern recognition to building inherently hybrid and efficient reasoning systems.

ideas.title: Compositional Neural Operators via Meta-Learned Sparse Routing
ideas.description: This approach refines adaptive neural operators by separating the learning of stable, reusable logical primitives from their query-specific composition. A meta-learning objective trains a core set of fixed, interpretable operator modules (e.g., for conjunction, negation, quantification) to be universally useful. For each query, a lightweight adaptive router learns to select and sparsely combine a subset of these core modules, dynamically constructing the required operator without modifying the primitives themselves. This ensures robust generalization, prevents catastrophic forgetting, and maintains the compositional structure necessary for logical reasoning across diverse tasks.

ideas.title: Formal Verification of Domain-Specific Data Curation for Provable AI Safety
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the co-design of specialized, curated training datasets and formal verification methods to build AI systems with provable safety properties for specific, high-assurance domains like healthcare or autonomous systems. Instead of verifying a model trained on broad data, the approach defines precise safety specifications first and then curates or synthesizes training data to satisfy the formal preconditions required for verification. This synergy allows the formal proofs to be more robust and complete, as the model's knowledge base is intentionally constrained and aligned with the domain's logical rules. The outcome is a methodology for generating 'verification-aware' datasets that yield models whose safe behavior is not just emergent but is a direct, provable consequence of their curated training foundation.

ideas.title: Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization for Energy-Efficient and Robust Models
ideas.description: This research proposes a training and inference framework that optimizes for energy efficiency under strict constraints for model robustness and fairness. Instead of a simple trade-off, the system uses a constrained optimization loop where energy consumption is minimized subject to verifiable thresholds for performance, adversarial accuracy, and bias metrics. This ensures the resulting models are not only energy-efficient but also reliable and equitable, preventing the failure mode where efficiency gains come at the cost of critical model safety. The approach includes novel loss functions and scheduling algorithms to navigate this complex design space effectively.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Interface Programs for Stable Neural-Symbolic Coordination
ideas.description: This research designs a hybrid architecture where a neural foundation model and a symbolic reasoner are connected via a meta-learned Interface Program (IP). The IP is a structured, parameterized module that translates neural activations into symbolic queries and formats symbolic outputs for neural consumption, governed by constraints that ensure robustness. The entire system—neural model, IP, and symbolic reasoner—is co-optimized through a meta-learning outer loop that trains the IP to facilitate stable gradient-based updates and efficient credit assignment across the non-differentiable boundary, enabling seamless end-to-end learning without assuming end-to-end differentiability.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Specialization: Pruning Models via Functional Modularity
ideas.description: This research adapts the evolutionary biological principle of gene duplication and specialization to the problem of creating efficient, task-specific models from large foundation models. In nature, a general-purpose gene can be duplicated, and the copies can diverge through subfunctionalization (partitioning the original roles) or neofunctionalization (acquiring new roles). Analogously, we propose to identify and duplicate key functional modules within a foundation model (e.g., transformer blocks or specific attention pathways). Each duplicated module then undergoes targeted, task-directed sparsification—pruning away connections and neurons not critical for a specific target task while preserving the core, transferable knowledge. This process of 'evolutionary specialization via modular sparsification' yields a diverse ecosystem of lean, specialized models, each optimized for a distinct function but all descended from and interoperable with the original foundational architecture, effectively scaling down compute and memory requirements while maintaining high task performance.

ideas.title: Architecture-Aware Failure Benchmarking for Robustness Diversity
ideas.description: This research proposes to develop a new class of evaluation benchmarks that are explicitly designed to diagnose how and why different neural network architectures fail. Instead of a one-size-fits-all robustness test, the benchmarks will generate a 'failure profile' for each architecture (e.g., Transformers, State Space Models, Hybrids). By cross-analyzing these profiles, we can identify which architectures are robust to the failure modes of others, moving beyond homogenized performance metrics to strategically cultivate architectural diversity. The ultimate goal is to provide a rigorous framework for building ensembles or selecting specialized models that, as a collective system, are resilient to a broader, known set of adversarial, out-of-distribution, and reasoning failures.

ideas.title: Edge-Adaptive KG Completion via Query-Guided Model Distillation
ideas.description: This research develops methods to distill the query-guided knowledge graph completion capability of systems like BetaE into minimal, efficient foundation models suitable for edge deployment. The core innovation is a distillation process where the large model's behavior in generating plausible facts from uncertain queries is used to train a small model to perform similar speculative reasoning locally. This enables edge devices to propose context-relevant KG augmentations in real-time, using local data, which can then be asynchronously validated and integrated into a central graph. The result is a scalable, privacy-preserving architecture for continuous knowledge graph growth that leverages edge intelligence.

ideas.title: Modular Neural Adaptation via a 'Cellular Compartment' Paradigm
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that transfer learning must occur primarily through fine-tuning all parameters of a pre-trained model. Drawing an analogy from synthetic biology, where new functions are added to cells via encapsulated genetic modules that interface without rewriting the core genome, we propose a 'cellular compartment' paradigm for neural adaptation. The solution involves developing adaptation methods where a frozen, core pre-trained model is extended by attaching task-specific, encapsulated neural modules. These modules interface through a standardized, limited set of activation ports or gating mechanisms, allowing for modular, non-destructive extension of model capabilities. This enables plug-and-play adaptation, rapid task switching, and composition of skills, moving beyond the monolithic approach of fine-tuning.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning with Ambiguous Logical Forms
ideas.description: This research direction develops hybrid neuro-symbolic architectures that relax end-to-end differentiability to handle queries where logical operators (AND, OR, implication) are themselves probabilistic or ambiguous. The system uses a foundation model as a substrate to generate candidate logical parses and their confidence scores, which are then passed to a non-differentiable symbolic reasoner that evaluates these interpretations against a knowledge base. A meta-controller, trained via reinforcement learning or black-box optimization, learns to refine the parsing and reasoning process based on final task performance, creating a closed-loop that jointly resolves logical ambiguity and executes sound inference. This enables applications in complex QA and decision support where user queries are inherently vague or context-dependent, moving beyond systems that require pre-defined, deterministic logical forms.

ideas.title: Architectural Efficiency for Emergence: A Biological Circuitry Analogy
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that massive scale is necessary for emergent capabilities in AI. By drawing an analogy from systems biology and neuroscience, where biological brains achieve immense complexity not through sheer neuron count but through highly efficient, specialized architectures (e.g., cortical columns, small-world networks), we adapt this solution to the AI context. The adapted idea proposes to design and test novel neural network architectures that incorporate principles of biological efficiency—such as enforced modularity, heterogeneous computational units, and specific connectivity patterns—to determine if these structural innovations can induce sophisticated, emergent behaviors at a fraction of the parameter count used in contemporary large-scale models. The outcome would be a new direction for efficient AI that prioritizes architectural intelligence over brute-force scaling.

ideas.title: Scale down by learning on graph summaries or sketches

ideas.title: Sparsely Gated Modular Specialization with Dynamic Task Routing
ideas.description: This approach transforms a large foundation model into a Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture where each expert is a distinct, sparsely activated module derived from a structured decomposition of the original parameters. Instead of pruning to create a single static specialist, a lightweight, trainable router network dynamically selects and combines the most relevant experts for a given input and task descriptor. This maintains the model's full knowledge base and compositional generalization while achieving computational efficiency comparable to a pruned model. The system is trained via a joint objective that encourages both expert specialization and robust routing, resulting in adaptable, task-aware models that avoid the brittleness of static pruning.

ideas.title: Immunological Adversarial Defense for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research draws an analogy from immunology, where controlled exposure to weakened pathogens (vaccination) builds adaptive, generalized immunity. The analogous solution involves presenting the immune system with safe threat variants to develop memory cells. Adapted to foundation model security, the idea is to create an 'Immunological Adversarial Defense' framework. This framework would systematically vaccinate foundation models by exposing them during training or fine-tuning to a carefully curated set of synthetically generated or strategically perturbed inputs that represent 'weakened' adversarial patterns. The goal is to stimulate the model's internal feature representations to develop a form of generalized robustness or 'immunological memory,' enabling it to recognize and resist a broader spectrum of adversarial attacks it has not explicitly been trained on, thereby improving security and adversarial robustness in a more scalable and fundamental way than current adversarial training methods.

ideas.title: Domain-Specific Data Curation via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecology's 'niche specialization' principle to challenge the assumption of broad data universality in AI. In ecology, specialists with traits curated for a specific environment outperform generalists within that niche. The adapted idea investigates whether defining a domain as an 'informational niche' and curating a specialized dataset—mirroring how a specialist species evolves—yields superior model performance for that domain compared to training on broad, generalist data. The work would involve developing frameworks to characterize informational niches and methods for curating niche-optimized training sets.

ideas.title: Apply Lyapunov Stability Analysis from Robotics to Ensure LLM Alignment
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from robotics and nonlinear control—Lyapunov stability analysis—to the problem of model alignment and stability. In robotics, engineers construct a Lyapunov function to formally prove a dynamic system will converge to a desired state. The analogous solution is to define a mathematical function that quantifies the behavioral 'distance' of a large model from a specified alignment objective. By then designing training loops, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) reward shaping, or constrained decoding strategies that act as control inputs to ensure this function consistently decreases, one can formally analyze and steer the model's dynamic behavior. This provides a theoretical framework for guaranteeing that model outputs remain stable, predictable, and aligned with human intent, even as the model undergoes further training or faces novel inputs.

ideas.title: Scale task complexity to real-world dialogue

ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Bidirectional Language-Knowledge Graph Pretraining

ideas.title: Architecture-Aware Data Curation for Specialized, Non-Homogenized Models
ideas.description: This research investigates the synergistic co-design of specialized data and alternative model architectures to break the dominant paradigm of training generalist models on broad data. It posits that the highest performance for a specific domain is achieved not just by curating data for that domain, but by curating data specifically for an architecture whose structural biases align with the domain's fundamental properties. This approach moves beyond simply swapping architectures or datasets in isolation, instead creating a closed-loop system where architectural choices inform data curation protocols and vice-versa, yielding models that are both highly capable in their niche and architecturally diverse, thus mitigating ecosystem-wide homogenization risks.

ideas.title: Hybrid Neuro-Symbolic Architectures via Constraint Satisfaction Interfaces
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of end-to-end differentiability by exploring hybrid models that integrate non-differentiable symbolic reasoning with foundation model substrates. The analogy is drawn from computational biology, specifically protein-ligand docking, where a non-differentiable conformational search algorithm (for discrete molecular poses) is interfaced with a differentiable neural scoring function (for binding affinity). The adapted solution proposes a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture where a discrete symbolic reasoner (the 'search algorithm') operates in parallel to a foundation model (the 'scoring function'). The symbolic component generates candidate reasoning steps, logical deductions, or action plans. The foundation model evaluates these candidates, providing a trainable, gradient-based scoring signal for their quality or contextual fit. This feedback guides the symbolic search's heuristics or pruning strategies, creating a closed-loop system that leverages the strengths of both paradigms without requiring the symbolic operations themselves to be differentiable. The interface is designed as a constraint satisfaction layer, where the foundation model learns to approximate constraints that the symbolic engine must satisfy, enabling joint optimization for tasks requiring discrete reasoning and continuous semantic understanding.

ideas.title: Heavy-Tailed Embeddings: From Financial Risk Modeling to Knowledge Graph Completion
ideas.description: This idea challenges the common bounded support or light-tailed distributional assumptions for embedding vectors by drawing an analogy from financial risk modeling. Just as finance moved from Gaussian to heavy-tailed distributions to model extreme market events, we propose to explore unbounded or semi-bounded, heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., multivariate t, hyperbolic secant) for embedding queries and entities in knowledge graph completion and related tasks. This adaptation aims to create a more robust embedding space that can naturally accommodate a 'long tail' of rare relationships and outlier entities, potentially leading to better generalization on complex, open-world queries where the true support of relations is unknown and not safely bounded.

ideas.title: Architectural Priors for Emergent Efficiency: A Controlled Search Within a Fixed Compute Budget
ideas.description: This research program tests the hypothesis that specific architectural inductive biases can induce emergent capabilities at significantly lower computational scale than standard dense Transformers. It establishes a fixed, sub-100 petaFLOP training compute budget and a target suite of emergent tasks (e.g., multi-step reasoning, code generation). Within this budget, it performs a systematic search across model families with strong priors like structured sparsity, state-space models, or causal graph neural networks, benchmarking them against a Transformer baseline trained with identical data and compute. The goal is to produce quantifiable evidence of which architectural choices, if any, can shift the scaling laws for emergence, moving the question from philosophical exploration to an engineering experiment.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Calibrated Alignment of Probabilistic and Textual Entity Representations
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple fusion to explicitly align the structured uncertainty of BetaE embeddings with the contextual semantics of language model embeddings via a contrastive alignment loss in a joint latent space. A context-aware gating mechanism dynamically modulates the influence of each modality for a given query, based on factors like relation type and entity descriptiveness. This ensures the combined representation is semantically coherent and properly calibrated, improving robustness on complex logical queries and for entities with sparse or ambiguous textual descriptions.

ideas.title: Apply causal inference to knowledge graph reasoning
ideas.description: Use causal discovery and inference methods to improve link prediction and reasoning on knowledge graphs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Open Graph Benchmark: Datasets for Machine Learning on Graphs

ideas.title: Heterogeneous Edge Intelligence via Dynamic Model Orchestration
ideas.description: This research develops methods to deploy foundation model capabilities at the edge not by compressing a single model, but by designing a system where a lightweight orchestrator manages a suite of specialized, efficient models. The orchestrator learns to decompose incoming tasks and intelligently route components to the most suitable specialized model or, for rare or complex cases, to a cloud-based foundation model via sparse queries. This approach guarantees robust performance across diverse edge conditions by maintaining access to broad capabilities when needed, while optimizing for latency, privacy, and efficiency through localized, specialized computation.

ideas.title: Auditable Real-Time Knowledge Graph Inference: Integrating Social Science Audits into BetaE Query Pipelines
ideas.description: This research develops a novel framework that integrates principles and methodologies from social science auditing directly into the indexing and approximation layers of a Beta Embedding (BetaE) system for real-time query answering on massive knowledge graphs. The core innovation is an 'audit-aware' indexing strategy that not only accelerates inference but also tags and monitors latent relational pathways for known or hypothesized social biases (e.g., gender, racial, cultural stereotypes). By doing so, the system can provide real-time fairness metrics alongside query answers, trigger warnings or alternative result explanations when biased inference patterns are detected, and allow for dynamic re-optimization of the inference process to mitigate discovered biases without sacrificing overall latency.

ideas.title: Apply PRODIGY to dynamic or temporal graphs
ideas.description: Adapt the prompt graph framework to handle graphs that evolve over time.

ideas.title: Apply contrastive learning to estimate GIB

ideas.sourceTitle: Graph Information Bottleneck

ideas.title: Federated Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction designs a federated learning framework where a foundation model is trained across decentralized, private knowledge graphs that evolve over time. It extends the BetaE framework with temporal logical operators to perform complex temporal reasoning queries (e.g., 'Which entities interacted before a private event occurred?') over the distributed, time-varying data. The core innovation is a protocol that allows the model to learn temporal relational patterns and answer queries by computing embeddings and performing logical operations across institutional boundaries, without exposing raw sequential data. This enables collaborative learning from dynamic, sensitive processes—like disease progression in separate hospitals or supply chain disruptions in competing companies—while maintaining strict data privacy and temporal fidelity.

ideas.title: Predict n-ary relations, not just binary
ideas.description: Extend the framework to complete hyper-relational facts involving more than two entities.

ideas.title: Investigate interpretability of joint representations

ideas.title: Modeling Embedding Correlations via Copula Theory
ideas.description: This research challenges the common assumption of independence between dimensions in Beta distribution embeddings. Drawing an analogy from quantitative finance's use of copulas for multivariate risk modeling, we propose a solution that separates the modeling of marginal distributions from dependency structures. The adapted idea is to apply copula theory to embed the correlation between dimensions: first, model each embedding dimension's marginal distribution as a Beta distribution, then use a copula function to model their joint behavior. This approach provides a flexible, principled framework for capturing complex interdependencies in embedding spaces, moving beyond simplistic independence assumptions and potentially improving model performance in tasks like uncertainty-aware representation learning or hierarchical classification.

ideas.title: Benchmark efficiency metrics like cost and latency

ideas.title: Quantifying and Optimizing the Specialization-Robustness Trade-off via Dynamic Data Curriculum Synthesis
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the simplistic 'specialized vs. broad' debate by developing a framework to quantify the 'effective breadth' of a dataset and its relationship to both in-domain performance and out-of-distribution robustness. It introduces a methodology to measure the point of diminishing returns for data curation within a specific domain and the complementary benefits of incorporating strategically selected broader data. The outcome is a systematic, evidence-based approach for synthesizing dynamic training curricula that blend data sources to construct models that are both highly competent for their target task and resilient to real-world distribution shifts.

ideas.title: Econometric Data Attribution and Valuation via Model Inversion
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that applies econometric models to quantify the marginal value of training data, but uses techniques from training data inference (e.g., membership inference, gradient matching, or reconstruction attacks) to provide the empirical, model-internal measurements required as inputs to those econometric models. Instead of valuing data based on generic proxies like scarcity or source, it directly measures a data point's causal impact on a specific model's final parameters and performance. This enables precise, auditable data valuation for compensation, data market design, and dataset curation, grounded in verifiable evidence of influence extracted from the model itself.

ideas.title: Invert direction of knowledge flow

ideas.title: Apply variational autoencoder for query generation

ideas.title: Failure-Mode-Guided Modular Sparsification for Robust Specialization
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using advanced evaluation benchmarks that probe model failure modes to inform the sparsification process of a large foundation model. Instead of pruning for generic efficiency, the method strategically removes or isolates network components (e.g., attention heads, MLP neurons) that are systematically linked to specific failures on a target task, as identified by the benchmark. This results in specialized, sparse modules that are not only computationally efficient but also inherently more robust, as their architecture is explicitly designed to avoid known failure pathways. The approach creates a virtuous cycle where evaluation drives specialization, and the resulting specialized models provide clearer substrates for further failure analysis.

ideas.title: Improve efficiency for larger knowledge graphs
ideas.description: Develop methods to scale the model to very large or dense knowledge graphs efficiently.

ideas.title: Apply graph neural network pruning techniques
ideas.description: Use graph pruning methods from network science to sparsify the joint graph before reasoning.
ideas.sourceTitle: QA-GNN: Reasoning with Language Models and Knowledge Graphs for Question Answering

ideas.title: Stabilized Unbounded Embeddings via Adaptive Regularization and Neighborhood Consistency
ideas.description: This research develops a method for using unbounded or heavy-tailed distributions in embedding models while maintaining training stability and retrieval robustness. The core innovation is a combined loss function with an adaptive regularizer that penalizes the ratio of a vector's norm to its within-class concentration, preventing dimensional collapse or explosion. Simultaneously, a neighborhood consistency objective ensures that similarity structure is preserved under the unbounded distribution, making nearest-neighbor search reliable. The system is evaluated on both semantic similarity and entity linking tasks, demonstrating improved representation capacity without the fragility of naive unbounded embeddings.

ideas.title: Adaptive Clinical Trial Design for Query-Guided KG Completion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from adaptive clinical trial design in biostatistics to the problem of using uncertain query answers for knowledge graph (KG) completion. In clinical trials, interim analyses of early, uncertain patient data are used to adaptively re-allocate resources and modify the trial's hypothesis-testing pathway. Analogously, for query-guided KG completion using BetaE, we propose a system that treats a complex logical query as a multi-stage exploration process. After executing an initial segment of the query, the system performs an 'interim analysis' on BetaE's probabilistic answer distributions. Based on this analysis—such as identifying high-variance entities or promising but uncertain relation paths—the system dynamically adjusts its subsequent reasoning strategy. It could allocate more computational budget to refine high-potential answers, prune unlikely inference branches, or suggest new factual candidates for KG augmentation from the most probable query interpretations, thereby making the fact-suggestion process more efficient and targeted.

ideas.title: Relax assumption of static graph structure
ideas.description: Create benchmarks and methods for learning on graphs that evolve rapidly with billions of changes.
ideas.sourceTitle: OGB-LSC: A Large-Scale Challenge for Machine Learning on Graphs

ideas.title: Genomic Paleontology: Reconstructing Training Data Ancestry via Model Phylogenetics
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original inverse problem idea via analogical reasoning from evolutionary biology. The analogous problem is reconstructing ancestral genomes from modern species, solved in paleogenomics through phylogenetic comparison and models of sequence evolution. We adapt this solution to machine learning by conceptualizing the training process as an evolutionary lineage where data points exert selective pressure, shaping a model's final parameter 'genome'. The adapted idea, 'Genomic Paleontology: Reconstructing Training Data Ancestry via Model Phylogenetics,' involves developing a framework to train an ensemble of models with strategically varied, known data diets to establish a controlled phylogeny. By then analyzing the parameter states and feature representations of a finalized 'target' model against this phylogenetic reference, we can apply statistical inference techniques to reconstruct the most probable influential data classes or even identify signatures of specific data lineages within the target model's training history, providing a powerful audit tool for data provenance.

ideas.title: Apply contrastive learning from vision

ideas.title: Improve security and adversarial robustness

ideas.title: Pareto-Optimal Neural Architecture Search for Energy-Accuracy Trade-offs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of Pareto-frontier optimization from evolutionary multi-objective engineering design to the problem of dual optimization for accuracy and energy in machine learning. In engineering, systems are optimized for multiple competing objectives (e.g., weight, cost, performance) by finding a set of 'Pareto-optimal' solutions where no objective can be improved without worsening another. Adapted here, the model training and neural architecture search process would be reformulated as a multi-objective optimization problem with two primary targets: predictive accuracy and total energy cost (encompassing both training and inference). The outcome would not be a single model, but a suite of models representing the optimal trade-off frontier, allowing practitioners to select a model based on their specific energy budget or accuracy requirement, fundamentally treating energy as a co-equal design constraint rather than a secondary metric.

ideas.title: Latent Dynamics Identification and Control for Verifiable Model Alignment
ideas.description: This research develops a methodology to identify a low-dimensional, interpretable latent state space where key alignment properties of a large model evolve over time or across prompts. Using interactive probing and system identification techniques, it learns a simplified but formally specified dynamics model in this latent space. A controller is then synthesized using control theory to steer these latent dynamics toward stable, aligned behavior. The approach's robustness is ensured by continuously validating the controller's effects on the full-scale model, closing the loop between the simplified analytical model and the complex reality.

ideas.title: Challenge that bias is purely architectural

ideas.sourceTitle: Bias and Generalization in Deep Generative Models: An Empirical Study

ideas.title: Essential Feature Distillation via Biological Metabolic Pathway Pruning
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Systems Biology and Metabolic Engineering, where minimal viable cells are created by preserving only essential metabolic pathways, to the problem of creating minimal viable foundation models. In the analogous field, scientists construct streamlined organisms by identifying core genes for survival and a target function, then performing genome reduction. We adapt this by conceptualizing a large model's architecture and learned representations as a computational metabolic network. The research will develop systematic methods to analyze the 'essentiality' of different model components (e.g., attention patterns, feature maps, layer interactions) for a target capability profile. The goal is to prune away redundant computational structures and distill the model down to a minimal, efficient 'chassis' that retains only the core pathways necessary for key functionalities, enabling effective deployment on edge devices with strict resource constraints.

ideas.title: Develop better evaluation metrics for scale

ideas.title: Dual: optimize for energy efficiency, not just accuracy

ideas.title: The Adaptation Efficiency Frontier: A Benchmarking Framework for Quantifying Paradigm Shifts Beyond Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This research proposes a concrete framework to move beyond advocating for alternative adaptation methods in the abstract. It first establishes a comprehensive multi-task benchmark suite designed to stress-test adaptation paradigms across dimensions of data efficiency, catastrophic forgetting, and cross-domain transfer. The core contribution is the 'Adaptation Efficiency Frontier,' a formal evaluation framework that quantifies the trade-offs between parameter efficiency, compute cost, performance gain, and base model retention for any adaptation method. By requiring all new paradigms—such as learned prompt banks, input-output routing networks, or parameter-efficient modular attachments—to be evaluated within this unified framework, the work forces a direct, empirical comparison with fine-tuning to identify under what conditions a paradigm shift is genuinely justified.

ideas.title: Temporal Resilience Reasoning for AI Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research fuses temporal logical querying from BetaE with ecological resilience models to create a novel framework for analyzing AI ecosystems. It extends BetaE with temporal operators to model how the ecosystem's knowledge graph—representing components, interactions, and dependencies—evolves under stress or shock. By integrating ecological resilience metrics (like resistance, recovery, and adaptive capacity), the framework can execute diagnostic queries to predict tipping points, identify critical fragile temporal pathways, and recommend interventions to enhance long-term ecosystem stability and robustness.

ideas.title: Decentralized Adversarial Co-Evolution for Robust Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research proposes a decentralized framework where multiple, independent nodes collaboratively build a foundation model while simultaneously acting as adversarial red teams and defensive blue teams for each other. The system formalizes a federated adversarial co-evolution process, where nodes contribute model updates and also generate and share novel attack vectors and corresponding robust training patches. This transforms the inherent heterogeneity and potential distrust of a decentralized system into a strength, creating a continuous, in-distribution stress test that proactively discovers and mitigates vulnerabilities across diverse data and threat landscapes. The outcome is a foundation model whose robustness is emergent from the collaborative security process, not just an added layer, making it inherently more secure and trustworthy than models developed in isolated, centralized environments.
